From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Protestant](/w/index.php?title=Protestant&redirect=no "Protestant"))

Major branch of Christianity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Lutherstadt_Wittenberg_09-2016_photo06.jpg/250px-Lutherstadt_Wittenberg_09-2016_photo06.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lutherstadt_Wittenberg_09-2016_photo06.jpg)

The door to [All Saints' Church](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Church,_Wittenberg "All Saints' Church, Wittenberg") in [Wittenberg](/wiki/Wittenberg "Wittenberg"), where [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") posted his *[Ninety-five Theses](/wiki/Ninety-five_Theses "Ninety-five Theses")* in 1517 detailing his concerns with what he saw as the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church")'s abuse and corruption. The *Ninety-five Theses* gave rise to Christian Protestantism as one of the world's primary religions, making Wittenberg the "cradle of Protestantism".

**Protestantism** is a form of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity")[[a]](#cite_note-1) that emphasizes [justification](/wiki/Justification_(theology) "Justification (theology)") of sinners [through faith alone](/wiki/Sola_fide "Sola fide"), the teaching that [salvation](/wiki/Salvation_in_Christianity "Salvation in Christianity") comes by unmerited [divine grace](/wiki/Grace_in_Christianity "Grace in Christianity"), the [priesthood of all believers](/wiki/Priesthood_of_all_believers "Priesthood of all believers"), and the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") as the sole infallible source of authority for Christian faith and practice.[[1]](#cite_note-WELS2014-2)[[2]](#cite_note-3) The [five *solae*](/wiki/Five_solae "Five solae") summarize the basic theological beliefs of mainstream Protestantism.

Protestants follow the [theological](/wiki/Christian_theology "Christian theology") tenets of the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"), a movement that began in the 16th century with the goal of reforming the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") from perceived [errors, abuses, and discrepancies](/wiki/Criticism_of_the_Catholic_Church "Criticism of the Catholic Church").[[3]](#cite_note-4)[[b]](#cite_note-5) The Reformation began in the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire")[[c]](#cite_note-6) in 1517, when [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") published his *[Ninety-five Theses](/wiki/Ninety-five_Theses "Ninety-five Theses")* as a reaction against abuses in the sale of [indulgences](/wiki/Indulgence "Indulgence") by the Catholic Church, which purported to offer the remission of the [temporal punishment](/wiki/Purgatory "Purgatory") of sins to their purchasers.[[4]](#cite_note-7) Luther's statements questioned the Catholic Church's role as negotiator between people and God, especially when it came to the indulgence arrangement, which in part granted people the power to purchase a certificate of pardon for the penalization of their sins. Luther argued against the practice of buying or earning forgiveness, claiming instead that salvation is a gift God gives to those who have faith.[[5]](#cite_note-auto2-8)

[Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") spread from Germany[[d]](#cite_note-9) into [Denmark–Norway](/wiki/Denmark%E2%80%93Norway "Denmark–Norway"), [Sweden](/wiki/History_of_Sweden_(1523%E2%80%931611) "History of Sweden (1523–1611)"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland_under_Swedish_rule "Finland under Swedish rule"), [Livonia](/wiki/Terra_Mariana "Terra Mariana"), and [Iceland](/wiki/History_of_Iceland#Iceland_under_Norwegian_and_Danish_kings_(1262–1944) "History of Iceland").[[6]](#cite_note-10) [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinist "Calvinist") churches spread in Germany,[[e]](#cite_note-11) [Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary_(1526%E2%80%931867) "Kingdom of Hungary (1526–1867)"), the [Netherlands](/wiki/Habsburg_Netherlands "Habsburg Netherlands"), [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Old_Swiss_Confederacy "Old Swiss Confederacy"), [France](/wiki/France_in_the_early_modern_period "France in the early modern period"), [Poland](/wiki/Crown_of_the_Kingdom_of_Poland "Crown of the Kingdom of Poland") and [Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania"), led by [Protestant Reformers](/wiki/Protestant_Reformers "Protestant Reformers") such as [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin"), [Huldrych Zwingli](/wiki/Huldrych_Zwingli "Huldrych Zwingli") and [John Knox](/wiki/John_Knox "John Knox").[[7]](#cite_note-12) The political separation of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") from the Catholic Church under [King Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII") began [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), bringing England and Wales into this broad Reformation movement, under the leadership of reformer [Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer"), whose work forged Anglican doctrine and identity.[[f]](#cite_note-13)

Protestantism is divided into various denominations on the basis of [theology](/wiki/Christian_theology "Christian theology") and [ecclesiology](/wiki/Protestant_ecclesiology "Protestant ecclesiology").[[8]](#cite_note-Hillerbrand-14) Protestants adhere to the concept of an [invisible church](/wiki/Church_invisible "Church invisible"), in contrast to the Catholic, the Eastern Orthodox Church, the Oriental Orthodox Churches, the [Assyrian Church of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East"), and the [Ancient Church of the East](/wiki/Ancient_Church_of_the_East "Ancient Church of the East"), which all understand themselves as the only original church—the "[one true church](/wiki/One_true_church "One true church")"—founded by [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity") (though certain Protestant denominations, including historic Lutheranism, hold to this position).[[9]](#cite_note-Karl_Heussi_1956_pp._317–319-15)[[10]](#cite_note-Remensnyder1893-16)[[11]](#cite_note-Frey1918-17) A majority of Protestants[[g]](#cite_note-19) are members of a handful of Protestant denominational families; [Anglicans/Episcopalians](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists"), [Calvinist/Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_Christianity "Reformed Christianity"),[[h]](#cite_note-21) [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), [Methodists](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") and [Moravians](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church").[[14]](#cite_note-pewforum1-22) Furthermore, [Adventists](/wiki/Adventism "Adventism"), [Pentecostals](/wiki/Pentecostals "Pentecostals"), [Quakers](/wiki/Quakerism "Quakerism"), [Plymouth Brethren](/wiki/Plymouth_Brethren "Plymouth Brethren"), independent churches also known as [Nondenominational Christianity](/wiki/Nondenominational_Christianity "Nondenominational Christianity"), and [Charismatic Christianity](/wiki/Charismatic_movement "Charismatic movement") can also be considered part of Protestantism broadly speaking. Independent/Non-Denominational churches are on the rise, having recently expanded rapidly throughout much of the world.[[15]](#cite_note-23)[[16]](#cite_note-24)[[17]](#cite_note-25)[[18]](#cite_note-26)[[19]](#cite_note-CSGC-27) These various movements, collectively labeled "popular Protestantism"[[i]](#cite_note-28) by scholars such as [Peter L. Berger](/wiki/Peter_L._Berger "Peter L. Berger"), have been called one of the contemporary world's most dynamic religious movements.[[20]](#cite_note-books.google.com-29)

Strictly and historically speaking however, Protestantism includes only the denominations that emerged directly during the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"), and does not include later branches such as [non-denominational Christianity](/wiki/Non-denominational_Christianity "Non-denominational Christianity") and others.[[21]](#cite_note-30)[[22]](#cite_note-Voorst2014-31) In 2004, Hans Hillerbrand estimated a total Protestant population of 833,457,000,[[23]](#cite_note-books.google.pl-32) while the [World Christian Database](/wiki/World_Christian_Encyclopedia "World Christian Encyclopedia") estimated 637,856,000 Protestants and 426,370,000 Independents (mostly non-denominational Pentecostals who do not self-identify as Protestants) in early 2026.[[24]](#cite_note-WorldChristianDatabase-33)[[25]](#cite_note-Independents-34)[[19]](#cite_note-CSGC-27)

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Gedaechtniskirche_Speyer_Sued.jpg/250px-Gedaechtniskirche_Speyer_Sued.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gedaechtniskirche_Speyer_Sued.jpg)

[Memorial Church](/wiki/Ged%C3%A4chtniskirche,_Speyer "Gedächtniskirche, Speyer"), finished and consecrated in 1904 in [Speyer](/wiki/Speyer "Speyer"), Germany, commemorates the [Protestation](/wiki/Protestation_at_Speyer "Protestation at Speyer").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Protestierende-Speyer_Worms_Lutherdenkmal_%2837a%29.jpg/250px-Protestierende-Speyer_Worms_Lutherdenkmal_%2837a%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Protestierende-Speyer_Worms_Lutherdenkmal_(37a).jpg)

*The Protesting Speyer*, part of the [Luther Monument](/wiki/Luther_Monument_(Worms) "Luther Monument (Worms)") in [Worms, Germany](/wiki/Worms,_Germany "Worms, Germany")

Six princes of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") and rulers of fourteen [Imperial Free Cities](/wiki/Imperial_Free_City "Imperial Free City"), who issued [a protest](/wiki/Protestation_at_Speyer "Protestation at Speyer") (or dissent) against the edict of the [Diet of Speyer (1529)](/wiki/Diet_of_Speyer_(1529) "Diet of Speyer (1529)"), were the first individuals to be called Protestants.[[26]](#cite_note-etymonline.com-35) The term *protestant*, though initially purely political in nature, later acquired a broader sense, referring to a member of any Western church which subscribed to the main Protestant principles.[[26]](#cite_note-etymonline.com-35) A Protestant is an adherent of any of those Christian bodies that separated from the Church of Rome during the Reformation, or of any group descended from them.[[27]](#cite_note-auto-36)

During the Reformation, the term *protestant* was hardly used outside of German politics. People who were involved in the religious movement used the word *evangelical* ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *evangelisch*). Gradually, *protestant* became a general term, meaning any adherent of the Reformation in the German-speaking area. It was ultimately somewhat taken up by Lutherans, even though [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") himself insisted on *Christian* or *evangelical* as the only acceptable names for individuals who professed faith in Christ. [French](/wiki/Huguenots "Huguenots") and [Swiss Protestants](/wiki/Reformation_in_Switzerland "Reformation in Switzerland") instead preferred the word *reformed*, which became a popular, neutral, and alternative name for Calvinists.

The word *evangelical*, which refers to [the gospel](/wiki/The_gospel "The gospel"), was widely used for those involved in the religious movement in the German-speaking area beginning in 1517.[[28]](#cite_note-37) *Evangelical* is still preferred among some of the historical Protestant denominations in the Lutheran, Calvinist, and United (Lutheran and Reformed) Protestant traditions in Europe, and those with strong ties to them. Above all the term is used by Protestant bodies in the [German-speaking area](/wiki/German-speaking_Europe "German-speaking Europe"), such as the [Protestant Church in Germany](/wiki/Protestant_Church_in_Germany "Protestant Church in Germany"). Thus, the [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") word **evangelisch** means Protestant, while the German **evangelikal**, refers to churches shaped by [Evangelicalism](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism"). The English word *evangelical* usually refers to [evangelical Protestant](/wiki/Evangelical_Protestant "Evangelical Protestant") churches, and therefore to a certain part of Protestantism rather than to Protestantism as a whole. The English word traces its roots back to the [Puritans](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans") in England, where Evangelicalism originated, and then was brought to the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").

Martin Luther always disliked the term *Lutheran*, preferring the term *evangelical*, which was derived from *euangelion*, a Greek word meaning "good news", i.e. "[gospel](/wiki/The_Gospel "The Gospel")".[[29]](#cite_note-OOE796-38) The followers of [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin"), [Huldrych Zwingli](/wiki/Huldrych_Zwingli "Huldrych Zwingli"), and other theologians linked to the [Reformed tradition](/wiki/Reformed_tradition "Reformed tradition") also began to use that term. To distinguish the two evangelical groups, others began to refer to the two groups as *Evangelical Lutheran* and *Evangelical Reformed*. Lutherans themselves began to use the term *Lutheran* in the middle of the 16th century, in order to distinguish themselves from other groups such as the [Philippists](/wiki/Philippists "Philippists") and [Calvinists](/wiki/Calvinists "Calvinists").

The [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") word **reformatorisch**, which roughly translates to English as "reformational" or "reforming", is used as an alternative for **evangelisch** in German, and is different from English *reformed* ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *reformiert*), which refers to churches shaped by ideas of [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin"), [Huldrych Zwingli](/wiki/Huldrych_Zwingli "Huldrych Zwingli"), and other Reformed theologians. Derived from the word "Reformation", the term emerged around the same time as *Evangelical* (1517) and *Protestant* (1529).

## Theology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Theology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Mikolow_protestant_church_pulpit.jpg/250px-Mikolow_protestant_church_pulpit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mikolow_protestant_church_pulpit.jpg)

Two central figures of the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"), [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") and [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin"), depicted on a church [pulpit](/wiki/Pulpit "Pulpit"); both Luther and Calvin emphasized making preaching a centerpiece of worship.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Lutherbibel.jpg/250px-Lutherbibel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lutherbibel.jpg)

The [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") translated into [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") by Martin Luther. In Protestantism, the Bible is the supreme authority.

Many experts have proposed criteria to determine whether a Christian denomination should be considered part of Protestantism. A common consensus approved by most of them is that if a Christian denomination is to be considered Protestant, it must acknowledge the following three fundamental principles of Protestantism.[[30]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_of_Protestantism-39)

### Sola Scriptura

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Sola Scriptura")]

Main article: [Sola scriptura](/wiki/Sola_scriptura "Sola scriptura")

The belief, emphasized by Luther, in the Bible as the highest source of authority for the church. The early churches of the Reformation believed in a critical, yet serious, reading of scripture and holding the Bible as a source of authority higher than that of [tradition](/wiki/Sacred_Tradition "Sacred Tradition"), though Lutherans cherish tradition for its role in maintaining order and transmitting the Gospel.[[31]](#cite_note-Jahn2014-40)[[32]](#cite_note-Webber1992-41) The many abuses that had occurred in the Western Church before the Protestant Reformation led the Reformers to reject certain Roman Catholic traditions.[[31]](#cite_note-Jahn2014-40) In the early 20th century, a less critical reading of the Bible developed in the United States—leading to a "[fundamentalist](/wiki/Christian_fundamentalism "Christian fundamentalism")" reading of Scripture. Christian fundamentalists read the Bible as the "inerrant, [infallible](/wiki/Biblical_infallibility "Biblical infallibility")" Word of God, as do the Catholic, Eastern Orthodox, Lutheran and Anglican churches, but interpret it in a [literalist](/wiki/Biblical_literalism "Biblical literalism") fashion without using the [historical-critical method](/wiki/Historical-critical_method "Historical-critical method"). Methodists and Anglicans differ from Lutherans and the Reformed on this doctrine as they teach *[prima scriptura](/wiki/Prima_scriptura "Prima scriptura")*, which holds that Scripture is the primary source for Christian doctrine, but that "tradition, experience, and reason" can nurture the Christian religion as long as they are in harmony with the Bible ([Protestant canon](/wiki/Protestant_Bible "Protestant Bible")).[[1]](#cite_note-WELS2014-2)[[33]](#cite_note-Humphrey2013-42)

[Quakers](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers") and [Methodists](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist") (inclusive of the [holiness movement](/wiki/Holiness_movement "Holiness movement")), as well as [Radical Pietists](/wiki/Radical_Pietism "Radical Pietism"), [Pentecostals](/wiki/Pentecostal "Pentecostal") and [Spiritual Christians](/wiki/Spiritual_Christianity "Spiritual Christianity") emphasize the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity") and personal closeness to God.[[34]](#cite_note-43)

There was also a time when scripture became the new religious imagery. [Reformed (Calvinist) theology](/wiki/Reformed_Christianity "Reformed Christianity") drew out iconoclastic events when John Calvin arrived in Geneva in 1536. Calvin inherited a city where the old medieval Christian world, with its rituals, images, and pilgrims were taken apart. After the events of the iconoclast in 1566, scripture began to take place of the religious images that were previously removed. These new text images turned scripture into a visual tool. The absence of traditional images created room for the joint idea of scripture with visual objects, generating a new imagery culture that focused on reading, understanding, and reflecting over observations.[[35]](#cite_note-44)

### Justification by faith alone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Justification by faith alone")]

Main article: [Sola fide](/wiki/Sola_fide "Sola fide")

Justification by faith alone is the belief that believers are [justified](/wiki/Justification_(theology) "Justification (theology)"), or pardoned for sin, solely through faith in [Christ](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") rather than a combination of faith and [good works](/wiki/Good_works "Good works"). For Protestants, good works are a necessary consequence rather than cause of justification.[[36]](#cite_note-SchaffHerzog-45) However, while justification is by faith alone, there is the position that faith is not *nuda fides*.[[37]](#cite_note-:0-46) John Calvin explained that "it is therefore faith alone which justifies, and yet the faith which justifies is not alone: just as it is the heat alone of the sun which warms the earth, and yet in the sun it is not alone."[[37]](#cite_note-:0-46) At the time of the justification of an individual, the Lutheran Churches teach that the process of [sanctification](/wiki/Sanctification_in_Christianity "Sanctification in Christianity") commences, which is defined as "the Holy Spirit’s work which follows justification through faith and consists of renewing the believer and bringing forth in him works of renewal."[[38]](#cite_note-Harstad1997-47) These [good works](/wiki/Good_works "Good works") done by Christians are rewarded by God.[[39]](#cite_note-Preus2025-48) Lutheran and Reformed Christians differ from Methodists in their view of the possibility of entire sanctification, with Methodists affirming it as a [second work of grace](/wiki/Second_work_of_grace "Second work of grace").[[40]](#cite_note-White2008-49)[[41]](#cite_note-Bucher2014-50)

### Universal priesthood of believers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Universal priesthood of believers")]

The universal [priesthood of believers](/wiki/Priesthood_of_all_believers "Priesthood of all believers") implies the right and duty of the Christian laity not only to read the Bible in the [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular"), but also to take part in the government and all the public affairs of the Church. It is opposed to the hierarchical system which puts the essence and authority of the Church in an exclusive priesthood, and which makes ordained priests the necessary mediators between God and the people.[[36]](#cite_note-SchaffHerzog-45) It is distinguished from the concept of the priesthood of all believers, which did not grant individuals the right to interpret the Bible apart from the Christian community at large because universal priesthood opened the door to such a possibility.[[42]](#cite_note-willsky-51) There are scholars who cite that this doctrine tends to subsume all distinctions in the church under a single spiritual entity.[[43]](#cite_note-52) Calvin referred to the universal priesthood as an expression of the relation between the believer and his God, including the freedom of a Christian to come to God through Christ without human mediation.[[44]](#cite_note-:1-53) He also maintained that this principle recognizes Christ as [prophet](/wiki/Prophet "Prophet"), priest, and king and that his priesthood is shared with his people.[[44]](#cite_note-:1-53) In this view, [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession") is not grounded in an unbroken [chain of ordination](/wiki/Episcopal_polity "Episcopal polity"), but in the [faithfulness](/wiki/Fidelity "Fidelity") of believers to the Word of God, expressed in both faith and practice.

### Trinity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Trinity")]

See also: [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity") and [Nontrinitarianism](/wiki/Nontrinitarianism "Nontrinitarianism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Shield-Trinity-Scutum-Fidei-English.svg/330px-Shield-Trinity-Scutum-Fidei-English.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Shield-Trinity-Scutum-Fidei-English.svg)

The [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity") is the belief that [God](/wiki/God_in_Christianity "God in Christianity") is one God in three persons: [the Father](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father"), [the Son](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") ([Jesus](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity")), and the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity").

Protestants who adhere to the [Nicene Creed](/wiki/Nicene_Creed "Nicene Creed") believe in three [persons](/wiki/Hypostasis_(philosophy_and_religion) "Hypostasis (philosophy and religion)") ([God the Father](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father"), [God the Son](/wiki/God_the_Son "God the Son"), and the [God the Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity")) as one God.

Movements that emerged around the time of the Protestant Reformation, but are not a part of Protestantism (e.g. [Unitarianism](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism")), reject the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity"). This often serves as a reason for exclusion of the [Unitarian Universalism](/wiki/Unitarian_Universalism "Unitarian Universalism"), [Oneness Pentecostalism](/wiki/Oneness_Pentecostalism "Oneness Pentecostalism"), and other movements from Protestantism by various observers. Unitarianism continues to have a presence mainly in [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"), England, and the United States.[[42]](#cite_note-willsky-51)

### Five solae

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Five solae")]

Main article: [Five solae](/wiki/Five_solae "Five solae")

The Five **solae** are five [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") phrases (or slogans) that emerged during the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") and summarize the reformers' basic differences in theological beliefs in opposition to the teaching of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") of the day.[[1]](#cite_note-WELS2014-2) The Latin word **sola** means "alone", "only", or "single".

The use of the phrases as summaries of teaching emerged over time during the Reformation, based on the overarching Lutheran and Reformed principle of **[sola scriptura](/wiki/Sola_scriptura "Sola scriptura")** (by scripture alone).[[1]](#cite_note-WELS2014-2) This idea contains the four main doctrines on the Bible: that its teaching is needed for salvation (necessity); that all the doctrine necessary for salvation comes from the Bible alone (sufficiency); that everything taught in the Bible is correct (inerrancy); and that, by the Holy Spirit overcoming sin, believers may read and understand truth from the Bible itself, though understanding is difficult, so the means used to guide individual believers to the true teaching is often mutual discussion within the church (clarity). The necessity and inerrancy were well-established ideas, garnering little criticism, though they later came under debate from outside during the [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"). The most contentious idea at the time though was the notion that anyone could simply pick up the Bible and learn enough to gain salvation.

The second main principle, **[sola fide](/wiki/Sola_fide "Sola fide")** (by faith alone), states that faith in Christ is sufficient alone for eternal salvation and justification. Though argued from scripture, and hence logically consequent to **sola scriptura**, this is the guiding principle of the work of Luther and the later reformers. Because **sola scriptura** placed the Bible as the only source of teaching, **sola fide** epitomizes the main thrust of the teaching the reformers wanted to get back to, namely the direct, close, personal connection between Christ and the believer, hence the reformers' contention that their work was Christocentric.

The other solas, as statements, emerged later, but the thinking they represent was also part of the early Reformation.

* **[Solus Christus](/wiki/Solus_Christus "Solus Christus")**: *Christ alone*

:   The Protestants characterize the dogma concerning the Pope as Christ's representative head of the Church on earth, the concept of works made meritorious by Christ, and the Catholic idea of a treasury of the merits of Christ and his saints, as a denial that Christ is the *only* mediator between [God](/wiki/God_in_Christianity "God in Christianity") and man.

* **[Sola Gratia](/wiki/Sola_Gratia "Sola Gratia")**: *Grace alone*

:   Protestants perceived Catholic salvation to be dependent upon the grace of God and the merits of one's own works. The reformers posited that salvation is a gift of God (i.e., God's act of free grace), dispensed by the Holy Spirit owing to the redemptive work of Jesus Christ alone.

* **[Soli Deo Gloria](/wiki/Soli_Deo_Gloria "Soli Deo Gloria")**: *Glory to God alone*

:   All glory is due to God alone since salvation is accomplished solely through his will and action. The reformers believed that human beings—even saints [canonized](/wiki/Canonization "Canonization") by the Catholic Church, the popes, and the ecclesiastical hierarchy—are not worthy of the glory.

### Christ's presence in the Eucharist

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Christ's presence in the Eucharist")]

Main article: [Eucharistic theology](/wiki/Eucharistic_theology "Eucharistic theology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Abendmahl-1547-LC.jpg/330px-Abendmahl-1547-LC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abendmahl-1547-LC.jpg)

A 1547 [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") depiction of the [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper") by [Lucas Cranach the Elder](/wiki/Lucas_Cranach_the_Elder "Lucas Cranach the Elder")

The Protestant movement began to diverge into several distinct branches in the mid-to-late 16th century. One of the central points of divergence was controversy over the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist"). Early Protestants rejected [transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation"), the Catholic [dogma](/wiki/Dogma "Dogma") of the [real presence of Christ in the Eucharist](/wiki/Real_presence_of_Christ_in_the_Eucharist "Real presence of Christ in the Eucharist") explaining the "transformation from bread and wine to the body and blood of Christ".[[45]](#cite_note-Sproul2006-54)[[46]](#cite_note-Pattison2017-55) They disagreed with one another concerning the presence of Christ and his body and blood in Holy Communion, with Lutherans affirming, along with Catholics, a corporeal presence,[[47]](#cite_note-MattoxRoeber2012-56)[[46]](#cite_note-Pattison2017-55) and the Reformed affirming a [real spiritual presence](/wiki/Lord%27s_Supper_in_Reformed_theology "Lord's Supper in Reformed theology").[[45]](#cite_note-Sproul2006-54)

* Lutherans hold that in the [Lord's Supper](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist"), the Body and Blood of Christ are [corporeally present](/wiki/Real_presence_of_Christ_in_the_Eucharist#Lutheran "Real presence of Christ in the Eucharist") "in, with, and under the form" of bread and wine,[[48]](#cite_note-57) a doctrine that the [Formula of Concord](/wiki/Formula_of_Concord "Formula of Concord") calls the [Sacramental union](/wiki/Sacramental_union "Sacramental union").[[49]](#cite_note-58) In the Eucharist, Lutherans teach that the faithful receive "the very body and blood of Christ."[[47]](#cite_note-MattoxRoeber2012-56)
* The [Reformed Churches](/wiki/Reformed_Christianity "Reformed Christianity") ([Continental Reformed](/wiki/Continental_Reformed "Continental Reformed"), [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian"), [Reformed Anglican](/wiki/Reformed_Anglican "Reformed Anglican") and [Congregationalist](/wiki/Congregationalist "Congregationalist") traditions) emphasize the [real *spiritual* presence](/wiki/Real_presence#Reformed "Real presence"), or *sacramental presence*, of Christ in the Lord's Supper, holding that "Christ is 'spiritually present' in the sacrament by the ministry of the Holy Spirit and is received by faith". At the time of the Reformation, Anglicans (and consequently Methodists) inherited the Reformed view of the Eucharist as a real spiritual presence,[[50]](#cite_note-Elwell2001-59)[[51]](#cite_note-González1987-60)[[52]](#cite_note-Mahoney2024-61)[[53]](#cite_note-Collins2025-62) and hold that the way that this real spiritual presence is manifested is a mystery.[[54]](#cite_note-Neal2014-63)
* Anabaptists hold a popular simplification of the [Zwinglian view](/wiki/Theology_of_Huldrych_Zwingli "Theology of Huldrych Zwingli"), without concern for theological intricacies as hinted at above, may see the Lord's Supper merely as a symbol of the shared faith of the participants, a commemoration of the facts of the crucifixion, and a reminder of their standing together as the body of Christ (a view referred to as *memorialism*).[[55]](#cite_note-Balmer2002-64)

### Other beliefs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Other beliefs")]

Protestants reject the Catholic doctrine of [papal supremacy](/wiki/Papal_supremacy "Papal supremacy"), and have variant views on the number of [sacraments](/wiki/Sacrament "Sacrament"), the [real presence](/wiki/Real_presence_of_Christ_in_the_Eucharist "Real presence of Christ in the Eucharist") of [Christ](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity") in the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist"), and matters of [ecclesiastical polity](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_polity "Ecclesiastical polity") and [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession").[[56]](#cite_note-Haffner1999-65)[[57]](#cite_note-66)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Protestantism](/wiki/History_of_Protestantism "History of Protestantism")

### Pre-Reformation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Pre-Reformation")]

See also: [Proto-Protestantism](/wiki/Proto-Protestantism "Proto-Protestantism") and [Girolamo Savonarola](/wiki/Girolamo_Savonarola "Girolamo Savonarola")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/e/e6/Lollardmap.jpg/330px-Lollardmap.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lollardmap.jpg)

Spread of [Lollardy](/wiki/Lollardy "Lollardy") in [Medieval England](/wiki/Medieval_England "Medieval England") and [Medieval Scotland](/wiki/Medieval_Scotland "Medieval Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/e/e1/Muttich%2C_Kamil_Vladislav_-_Mistr_Jan_Hus_na_hranici_v_Kostnici_1415.jpg)](/wiki/File:Muttich,_Kamil_Vladislav_-_Mistr_Jan_Hus_na_hranici_v_Kostnici_1415.jpg)

The execution of [Jan Hus](/wiki/Jan_Hus "Jan Hus") in 1415

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Girolamo-Savonarola----w.jpg/330px-Girolamo-Savonarola----w.jpg)](/wiki/File:Girolamo-Savonarola----w.jpg)

[Girolamo Savonarola](/wiki/Girolamo_Savonarola "Girolamo Savonarola")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Portret_van_Johan_Wessel_Gansfort%2C_RP-P-1906-1520.jpg/330px-Portret_van_Johan_Wessel_Gansfort%2C_RP-P-1906-1520.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portret_van_Johan_Wessel_Gansfort,_RP-P-1906-1520.jpg)

[Wessel Gansfort](/wiki/Wessel_Gansfort "Wessel Gansfort")

Many of the individual ideas that were taken up by various reformers had historical pre-cursors; however, calling them *proto-reformers* is controversial, as often their theology also had components that are not associated with later Protestants, or that were asserted by some Protestants but denied by others, or that were only superficially similar.

One of the earliest persons to be praised as a Protestant forerunner is [Jovinian](/wiki/Jovinian "Jovinian"), who lived in the fourth century AD. He attacked [monasticism](/wiki/Monasticism "Monasticism"), [ascetism](/wiki/Asceticism "Asceticism") and believed that a saved believer can never be overcome by Satan.[[58]](#cite_note-67)

In the 9th century, the theologian [Gottschalk of Orbais](/wiki/Gottschalk_of_Orbais "Gottschalk of Orbais") was condemned for heresy by the Catholic Church. Gottschalk believed that the salvation of Jesus was limited and that his redemption was only for the elect.[[59]](#cite_note-68) The theology of Gottschalk anticipated the Protestant reformation.[[60]](#cite_note-69)[[61]](#cite_note-70)[*[self-published source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] [Ratramnus](/wiki/Ratramnus "Ratramnus") also defended the theology of Gottschalk and denied the real presence of Christ in the Eucharist; his writings also influenced the later Protestant reformation.[[62]](#cite_note-71) [Claudius of Turin](/wiki/Claudius_of_Turin "Claudius of Turin") in the 9th century also held Protestant ideas, such as [faith alone](/wiki/Sola_fide "Sola fide") and rejection of the supremacy of Peter.[[63]](#cite_note-72)

In the late 1130s, [Arnold of Brescia](/wiki/Arnold_of_Brescia "Arnold of Brescia"), an Italian [canon regular](/wiki/Canon_regular "Canon regular") became one of the first theologians to attempt to reform the Catholic Church. After his death, his teachings on [apostolic poverty](/wiki/Apostolic_poverty "Apostolic poverty") gained currency among [Arnoldists](/wiki/Arnoldists "Arnoldists"), and later more widely among [Waldensians](/wiki/Waldensians "Waldensians") and the [Spiritual Franciscans](/wiki/Spiritual_Franciscans "Spiritual Franciscans"), though no written word of his has survived the official condemnation. In the early 1170s, [Peter Waldo](/wiki/Peter_Waldo "Peter Waldo") founded the Waldensians. He advocated an interpretation of the Gospel that led to conflicts with the Catholic Church. By 1215, the Waldensians were declared heretical and subject to persecution. Despite that, the movement continues to exist to this day in Italy, as [a part of the wider Reformed tradition](/wiki/Waldensian_Evangelical_Church "Waldensian Evangelical Church").

In the 1370s, Oxford theologian and priest [John Wycliffe](/wiki/John_Wycliffe "John Wycliffe")—later dubbed the "Morning Star of Reformation"—started his activity as an English reformer. He rejected papal authority over secular power (in that any person in mortal sin lost their authority and should be resisted: a priest with possessions, such as a pope, was in such grave sin), may have [translated the Bible](/wiki/Wycliffe%27s_Bible "Wycliffe's Bible") into [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") English, and preached anticlerical and biblically centred reforms. His rejection of a real divine presence in the elements of the Eucharist foreshadowed Huldrych Zwingli's similar ideas in the 16th century. Wycliffe's admirers came to be known as "[Lollards](/wiki/Lollardy "Lollardy")".[[64]](#cite_note-73)

Beginning in the first decade of the 15th century, [Jan Hus](/wiki/Jan_Hus "Jan Hus")—a Catholic priest, Czech reformist and professor—influenced by John Wycliffe's writings, founded the [Hussite](/wiki/Hussite "Hussite") movement. He strongly advocated his reformist [Bohemian](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia") religious denomination. He was [excommunicated](/wiki/Excommunication "Excommunication") and [burned at the stake](/wiki/Burned_at_the_stake "Burned at the stake") in [Constance](/wiki/Konstanz "Konstanz"), [Bishopric of Constance](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Constance "Prince-Bishopric of Constance"), in 1415 by secular authorities for unrepentant and persistent heresy. After his execution, a revolt erupted. Hussites defeated five continuous crusades proclaimed against them by the [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope").

Later theological disputes caused a split within the Hussite movement. [Utraquists](/wiki/Utraquism "Utraquism") maintained that both the bread and the wine should be administered to the people during the Eucharist. Another major faction were the [Taborites](/wiki/Taborites "Taborites"), who opposed the Utraquists in the [Battle of Lipany](/wiki/Battle_of_Lipany "Battle of Lipany") during the [Hussite Wars](/wiki/Hussite_Wars "Hussite Wars"). There were two separate parties among the Hussites: moderate and radical movements. Other smaller regional Hussite branches in [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia") included [Adamites](/wiki/Adamites "Adamites"), [Orebites](/wiki/Orebites "Orebites"), [Orphans](/wiki/Sirotci "Sirotci"), and Praguers.

The Hussite Wars concluded with the victory of [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor") [Sigismund](/wiki/Sigismund,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Sigismund, Holy Roman Emperor"), his Catholic allies and moderate Hussites and the defeat of the radical Hussites. Tensions arose as the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War") reached Bohemia in 1620. Both moderate and radical Hussitism was increasingly persecuted by Catholics and Holy Roman Emperor's armies.

In the 14th century, a German mysticist group called the [Gottesfreunde](/wiki/Friends_of_God "Friends of God") criticized the Catholic church and its corruption. Many of their leaders were executed for attacking the Catholic church and they believed that God's judgement would soon come upon the church. The Gottesfreunde were a democratic lay movement and forerunner of the Reformation and put heavy stress of holiness and piety,[[65]](#cite_note-74)

Starting in 1475, an Italian Dominican friar [Girolamo Savonarola](/wiki/Girolamo_Savonarola "Girolamo Savonarola") was calling for a Christian renewal. Later on, Martin Luther himself read some of the friar's writings and praised him as a martyr and forerunner whose ideas on faith and grace anticipated Luther's own doctrine of justification by faith alone.[[66]](#cite_note-:02-75)

Some of Hus' followers founded the [Unitas Fratrum](/wiki/Unitas_Fratrum "Unitas Fratrum")—"Unity of the Brethren"—which was renewed under the leadership of [Count Nicolaus von Zinzendorf](/wiki/Zinzendorf "Zinzendorf") in [Herrnhut](/wiki/Herrnhut "Herrnhut"), [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony"), in 1722 after its almost total destruction in the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War") and the [Counterreformation ("Catholic Reformation")](/wiki/Counter-Reformation#Politics "Counter-Reformation"). Today, it is usually referred to in English as the [Moravian Church](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church") and in German as the [Herrnhuter Brüdergemeine](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church").

In the 15th century, three German theologians anticipated the reformation: [Wessel Gansfort](/wiki/Wessel_Gansfort "Wessel Gansfort"), [Johann Ruchat von Wesel](/wiki/Johann_Ruchrat_von_Wesel "Johann Ruchrat von Wesel"), and [Johannes von Goch](/wiki/Johannes_von_Goch "Johannes von Goch"). They held ideas such as [predestination](/wiki/Predestination "Predestination"), [sola scriptura](/wiki/Sola_scriptura "Sola scriptura"), and the [church invisible](/wiki/Church_invisible "Church invisible"), and denied the Catholic view on justification and the authority of the Pope, also questioning [monasticism](/wiki/Christian_monasticism "Christian monasticism").[[67]](#cite_note-76) Many protestant reformers also identified the pope as AntiChrist  Some reasons as to why, include that in the canon law [Decretum Gratiani](/wiki/Decretum_Gratiani "Decretum Gratiani") Distinctio 96 Chapter "satis evideter", protestants considered the pope claiming illegitimate divine authority including the name "god" which rightly belonged to God. Some also took issue with the interpretation of canon law by some Catholics such as the canonist Zelensinus De Cassanus who wrote the phrase "[Dominum Deum Nostrum Papam](/wiki/Dominum_Deum_Nostrum_Papam "Dominum Deum Nostrum Papam")" or others such as "Deus in Terra" (God on earth) to refer to the pope.[[68]](#cite_note-77)[[69]](#cite_note-78)[[70]](#cite_note-79)[[71]](#cite_note-80)

Wessel Gansfort also denied [transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation") and anticipated the Lutheran view of justification by faith alone.[[72]](#cite_note-81)

### Reformation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Reformation")]

Main article: [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/HolyRomanEmpire_1618.png/330px-HolyRomanEmpire_1618.png)](/wiki/File:HolyRomanEmpire_1618.png)

The [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") began as an attempt to reform the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").

On 31 October 1517, [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") allegedly nailed his [Ninety-five Theses](/wiki/Ninety-five_Theses "Ninety-five Theses"), also known as the Disputation on the Power of Indulgences, on the door of the [All Saints' Church](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Church,_Wittenberg "All Saints' Church, Wittenberg") in [Wittenberg](/wiki/Wittenberg "Wittenberg"), Germany, detailing doctrinal and practical abuses of the Catholic Church, especially the selling of [indulgences](/wiki/Indulgence "Indulgence"). The theses debated and criticized many aspects of the Church and the papacy, including the practice of [purgatory](/wiki/Purgatory#Protestantism "Purgatory"), [particular judgment](/wiki/Particular_judgment#Reformation_concepts "Particular judgment"), and the authority of the pope. Luther would later write works against the Catholic devotion to [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary"), the intercession of and devotion to the saints, mandatory clerical celibacy, monasticism, the authority of the pope, the ecclesiastical law, censure and [excommunication](/wiki/Excommunication "Excommunication"), the role of secular rulers in religious matters, the relationship between Christianity and the law, good works, and the sacraments.[[73]](#cite_note-Schofield122-82)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/William_the_Silent_16th_century.jpg/250px-William_the_Silent_16th_century.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_the_Silent_16th_century.jpg)

William the Silent (1533–1584, also known as William of Orange) was the leader of the Dutch Revolt in the Eighty Years' War in the Netherlands; first politically from 1559–1568, then militarily from 1568–1584. He is considered one of the most significant figures in Dutch history, known as the Father of the Fatherland, including in European history of the 16th century.

William was a stadtholder (administrator/steward) for King Philip II of Spain (1556–1598) who ruled the Low Countries (Netherlands) after his father, Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor (1519–1556) resigned. Charles V instituted the Inquisition into the Low Countries to subdue the heresies of the Protestant Reformation (according to him), as Philip II carried on his policies but with much greater motivation and gravity. He first granted Cardinal Antoine Perrenot de Granvelle (1517–1586) as head to the Inquisition and then gave the duty to Fernando Álvarez de Toledo, 3rd Duke of Alba (1507–1582) in 1567, whose oppressions resulted in the thousands of deaths and William's flight to his hometown of Dillenburg.

William developed an uprising, and returned in 1568, leading the Dutch Protestant forces against those of Catholic Spain until his assassination in 1584. The role of leader then went to his son, Maurice of Orange (1567–1625), followed by another son, Frederick Henry (1584–1647), who continued his father's vision until his death one year before the end of the Eighty Years' War and complete Dutch independence.[[74]](#cite_note-menprotestant-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Martin-Bucer_1.jpg/250px-Martin-Bucer_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Martin-Bucer_1.jpg)

Martin Bucer (1491–1551) was a German reformer and theologian who was a Dominican friar and priest until his conversion to the Lutheranism in 1518. He is most famous for his focus on unity among all Christians, and therefore, he never settled his own sect but influenced many instead.
Similar to other reformers, Bucer was attracted to the works of the humanist theologian and scholar Desiderius Erasmus (1466–1536) before discovering Martin Luther speak in 1518. Erasmus' humanism persuaded Bucer that Luther's beliefs were legit, even though Luther and Erasmus disagreed on many key issues. After departing from the Dominican order in 1521, Bucer preached the Lutheran faith and welcomed the new movement, marrying the former nun Elisabeth Silbereisen in 1522.[[74]](#cite_note-menprotestant-83)

The [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") was a triumph of literacy and the new [printing press](/wiki/Printing_press "Printing press") invented by [Johannes Gutenberg](/wiki/Johannes_Gutenberg "Johannes Gutenberg").[[75]](#cite_note-Cameron-84)[[j]](#cite_note-86) Luther's translation of the Bible into German was a decisive moment in the spread of literacy, and stimulated as well the printing and distribution of religious books and pamphlets. From 1517 onward, religious pamphlets flooded much of Europe.[[77]](#cite_note-Edwards-87)[[k]](#cite_note-89) During the Reformation, the Bible was translated into the native tongues of various European peoples, granting the common man access to sacred scripture, rather than relying solely on the Church's Latin version and interpretation. These translations, once forbidden, stirred a profound shift in religious thought, literacy, education, and the spread of Protestant ideas across parts of Holy Roman Empire and independent kingdoms.[[79]](#cite_note-90) Reformers such as Martin Luther translated the Bible into German, making it accessible to ordinary German speakers.[[80]](#cite_note-91) William Tyndale produced an English translation, although his efforts were met with resistance and he was captured in Antwerp before it was completed. Condemned for heresy, he was executed by strangulation and then burned at the stake at Vilvoorde in 1536.[[81]](#cite_note-92)[[82]](#cite_note-93) Similar translations into other native tongues took place across Europe.[[83]](#cite_note-94)

Following the excommunication of Luther and condemnation of the Reformation by the Pope, the work and writings of [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin") were influential in establishing a loose consensus among various groups in Switzerland, Scotland, Hungary, Germany and elsewhere. After the expulsion of its Bishop in 1526, and the unsuccessful attempts of the [Bern](/wiki/Bern "Bern") reformer [William Farel](/wiki/William_Farel "William Farel"), Calvin was asked to discipline the city of [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva "Geneva"). His *Ordinances of 1541* involved a collaboration of Church affairs with the city council and consistory to bring morality to all areas of life. After the establishment of the Geneva academy in 1559, Geneva became the unofficial capital of the Protestant movement, providing refuge for Protestant exiles from all over Europe and educating them as Calvinist missionaries. The faith continued to spread after Calvin's death in 1563.

Protestantism also spread from the German lands into France, where the Protestants were nicknamed [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenots "Huguenots"). Despite heavy persecution, the Reformed tradition made steady progress across large sections of the nation, appealing to people alienated by the obduracy and the complacency of the Catholic establishment. French Protestantism came to acquire a distinctly political character, made all the more obvious by the conversions of nobles during the 1550s. This established the preconditions for a series of conflicts, known as the [French Wars of Religion](/wiki/French_Wars_of_Religion "French Wars of Religion"). The civil wars gained impetus with the sudden death of [Henry II of France](/wiki/Henry_II_of_France "Henry II of France") in 1559. Atrocity and outrage became the defining characteristics of the time, illustrated at their most intense in the [St. Bartholomew's Day massacre](/wiki/St._Bartholomew%27s_Day_massacre "St. Bartholomew's Day massacre") of August 1572, when the Catholic party annihilated between 30,000 and 100,000 Huguenots across France. The wars only concluded when [Henry IV of France](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France "Henry IV of France") issued the [Edict of Nantes](/wiki/Edict_of_Nantes "Edict of Nantes"), promising official toleration of the Protestant minority, but under highly restricted conditions. Catholicism remained the official [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion"), and the fortunes of French Protestants gradually declined over the next century, culminating in [Louis XIV's](/wiki/Louis_XIV_of_France "Louis XIV of France") [Edict of Fontainebleau](/wiki/Edict_of_Fontainebleau "Edict of Fontainebleau") which revoked the Edict of Nantes and made Catholicism the sole legal religion. In response to the Edict of Fontainebleau, [Frederick William I, Elector of Brandenburg](/wiki/Frederick_William_I,_Elector_of_Brandenburg "Frederick William I, Elector of Brandenburg") declared the [Edict of Potsdam](/wiki/Edict_of_Potsdam "Edict of Potsdam"), giving free passage to Huguenot refugees. In the late 17th century, many Huguenots fled. A significant community in France remained in the [Cévennes](/wiki/C%C3%A9vennes "Cévennes") region.

Parallel to events in Germany, a movement began in Switzerland under the leadership of Huldrych Zwingli. Although the two movements agreed on many issues of theology, some unresolved differences kept them separate. A long-standing resentment between the German states and the [Swiss Confederation](/wiki/Swiss_Confederation "Swiss Confederation") led to heated debate over how much Zwingli owed his ideas to Lutheranism. The German Prince [Philip of Hesse](/wiki/Philip_of_Hesse "Philip of Hesse") saw potential in creating an alliance between Zwingli and Luther. A meeting was held in his castle in 1529, now known as the [Colloquy of Marburg](/wiki/Colloquy_of_Marburg "Colloquy of Marburg"), which has become infamous for its failure. The two men could not come to any agreement due to their disputation over one key doctrine.

In 1534, [King Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England") put an end to all papal jurisdiction in [England](/wiki/England "England"), after the Pope failed to [annul](/wiki/Annul "Annul") his marriage to [Catherine of Aragon](/wiki/Catherine_of_Aragon "Catherine of Aragon") (due to political considerations involving the Holy Roman Emperor);[[84]](#cite_note-95) this opened the door to reformational ideas. Later on, King Henry rejected the Pope's authority, instead of creating and accepting authority over the Church of England, a type of hybrid church that clashed together some Catholic doctrine and some Protestant ethics.[[5]](#cite_note-auto2-8) Within the next 20 years, there was religious disturbance in England as Queen Mary (1553–1558) restored Catholicism in England while persecuting and exiling Protestants, only to have Queen Elizabeth I and her Parliament try to lead the country back toward Protestantism during her reign (1558–1603).[[5]](#cite_note-auto2-8) Reformers in the Church of England alternated between sympathies for ancient Catholic tradition and more Reformed principles, gradually developing into a tradition considered a middle way (**via media**) between the Catholic and Protestant traditions. The English Reformation followed a particular course. The different character of the [English Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation") came primarily from the fact that it was driven initially by the political necessities of Henry VIII. King Henry decided to remove the Church of England from the authority of Rome. In 1534, the Act of Supremacy recognized Henry as "the only Supreme Head on earth of the Church of England". Between 1535 and 1540, under [Thomas Cromwell](/wiki/Thomas_Cromwell "Thomas Cromwell"), the policy known as the [Dissolution of the Monasteries](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Monasteries "Dissolution of the Monasteries") was put into effect. Following a brief Catholic restoration during the reign of Mary I, a loose consensus developed during the reign of [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I"). The [Elizabethan Religious Settlement](/wiki/Elizabethan_Religious_Settlement "Elizabethan Religious Settlement") largely formed Anglicanism into a distinctive church tradition. The compromise was uneasy and was capable of veering between extreme Calvinism on the one hand and Catholicism on the other. It was relatively successful until the Puritan Revolution or [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") in the 17th century.

The success of the Counterreformation ("Catholic Reformation") on the Continent and the growth of a [Puritan party](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans") dedicated to further Protestant reform polarized the [Elizabethan Age](/wiki/Elizabethan_Age "Elizabethan Age"). The early Puritan movement was a movement for reform in the Church of England whose proponents desired for the Church of England to resemble more closely the Protestant churches of Europe, especially that of Geneva. The later Puritan movement, often referred to as [dissenters](/wiki/Dissenters "Dissenters") and [nonconformists](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)"), eventually led to the formation of various Reformed denominations.

The [Scottish Reformation](/wiki/Scottish_Reformation "Scottish Reformation") of 1560 decisively shaped the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland").[[85]](#cite_note-96) The Reformation in Scotland culminated ecclesiastically in the establishment of a church along Reformed lines, and politically in the triumph of English influence over that of France. John Knox is regarded as the leader of the Scottish Reformation. The [Scottish Reformation Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Reformation_Parliament "Scottish Reformation Parliament") of 1560 repudiated the pope's authority by the [Papal Jurisdiction Act 1560](/wiki/Papal_Jurisdiction_Act_1560 "Papal Jurisdiction Act 1560"), forbade the celebration of the Mass and approved a Protestant Confession of Faith. It was made possible by a revolution against French hegemony under the regime of the regent [Mary of Guise](/wiki/Mary_of_Guise "Mary of Guise"), who had governed Scotland in the name of her absent [daughter](/wiki/Mary,_Queen_of_Scots "Mary, Queen of Scots").

Some of the most important activists of the Protestant Reformation included [Jacobus Arminius](/wiki/Jacobus_Arminius "Jacobus Arminius"), [Theodore Beza](/wiki/Theodore_Beza "Theodore Beza"), [Martin Bucer](/wiki/Martin_Bucer "Martin Bucer"), [Andreas von Carlstadt](/wiki/Andreas_von_Carlstadt "Andreas von Carlstadt"), [Heinrich Bullinger](/wiki/Heinrich_Bullinger "Heinrich Bullinger"), [Balthasar Hubmaier](/wiki/Balthasar_Hubmaier "Balthasar Hubmaier"), [Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer"), [William Farel](/wiki/William_Farel "William Farel"), [Thomas Müntzer](/wiki/Thomas_M%C3%BCntzer "Thomas Müntzer"), [Laurentius Petri](/wiki/Laurentius_Petri "Laurentius Petri"), [Olaus Petri](/wiki/Olaus_Petri "Olaus Petri"), [Philipp Melanchthon](/wiki/Philipp_Melanchthon "Philipp Melanchthon"), [Menno Simons](/wiki/Menno_Simons "Menno Simons"), [Louis de Berquin](/wiki/Louis_de_Berquin "Louis de Berquin"), [Primož Trubar](/wiki/Primo%C5%BE_Trubar "Primož Trubar") and [John Smyth](/wiki/John_Smyth_(Baptist_minister) "John Smyth (Baptist minister)").

In the course of this religious upheaval, the [German Peasants' War](/wiki/German_Peasants%27_War "German Peasants' War") of 1524–25 swept through the [Bavarian](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), [Thuringian](/wiki/Thuringia "Thuringia") and [Swabian](/wiki/Swabia "Swabia") principalities. After the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War") in the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries") and the [French Wars of Religion](/wiki/French_Wars_of_Religion "French Wars of Religion"), the confessional division of the states of the Holy Roman Empire eventually erupted in the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War") between 1618 and 1648. It devastated much of [Germany](/wiki/Early_Modern_history_of_Germany "Early Modern history of Germany"), killing between 25% and 40% of its population.[[86]](#cite_note-97) The main tenets of the [Peace of Westphalia](/wiki/Peace_of_Westphalia "Peace of Westphalia"), which ended the Thirty Years' War, were:

* All parties would recognize the [Peace of Augsburg](/wiki/Peace_of_Augsburg "Peace of Augsburg") of 1555, by which each prince would have the right to determine the religion of his own state, the options being Catholicism, Lutheranism, and now Calvinism. (the principle of *[cuius regio, eius religio](/wiki/Cuius_regio,_eius_religio "Cuius regio, eius religio")*)
* Christians living in principalities where their denomination was *not* the established church were guaranteed the right to practice their faith in public during allotted hours and in private at their will.
* The treaty also effectively ended the papacy's pan-European political power. [Pope Innocent X](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_X "Pope Innocent X") declared the treaty "null, void, invalid, iniquitous, unjust, damnable, reprobate, inane, empty of meaning and effect for all times" in his bull **Zelo Domus Dei**. European sovereigns, Catholic and Protestant alike, ignored his verdict.[[87]](#cite_note-ODCCWestphalia-98)

Peak of the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") and beginning of the Counterreformation ("Catholic Reformation") (1545–1620)

### Post-Reformation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Post-Reformation")]

See also: [Great Awakening](/wiki/Great_Awakening "Great Awakening"), [Restorationism](/wiki/Restorationism "Restorationism"), and [Azusa Street Revival](/wiki/Azusa_Street_Revival "Azusa Street Revival")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/1839-meth.jpg/250px-1839-meth.jpg)](/wiki/File:1839-meth.jpg)

An 1839 [Methodist](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") camp meeting during the [Second Great Awakening](/wiki/Second_Great_Awakening "Second Great Awakening") in the U.S.

The Great Awakenings were periods of rapid and dramatic religious revival in Anglo-American religious history.

The [First Great Awakening](/wiki/First_Great_Awakening "First Great Awakening") was an evangelical and revitalization movement that swept through Protestant Europe and [British America](/wiki/British_America "British America"), especially the [American colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") in the 1730s and 1740s, leaving a permanent impact on [American Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism_in_the_United_States "Protestantism in the United States"). It resulted from powerful preaching that gave listeners a sense of deep personal revelation of their need of salvation by Jesus Christ. Pulling away from ritual, ceremony, sacramentalism and hierarchy, it made Christianity intensely personal to the average person by fostering a deep sense of spiritual conviction and redemption, and by encouraging introspection and a commitment to a new standard of personal morality.[[88]](#cite_note-99)

The [Second Great Awakening](/wiki/Second_Great_Awakening "Second Great Awakening") began around 1790. It gained momentum by 1800. After 1820, membership rose rapidly among [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") and [Methodist](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") congregations, whose preachers led the movement. It was past its peak by the late 1840s. It has been described as a reaction against skepticism, [deism](/wiki/Deism "Deism"), and [rationalism](/wiki/Rationalism "Rationalism"), although why those forces became pressing enough at the time to spark revivals is not fully understood.[[89]](#cite_note-100) It enrolled millions of new members in existing [evangelical](/wiki/Evangelical "Evangelical") denominations and led to the formation of new denominations.

The [Third Great Awakening](/wiki/Third_Great_Awakening "Third Great Awakening") refers to a hypothetical historical period that was marked by religious activism in [American history](/wiki/American_history "American history") and spans the late 1850s to the early 20th century.[[90]](#cite_note-101) It affected [pietistic](/wiki/Pietistic "Pietistic") Protestant denominations and had a strong element of social activism.[[91]](#cite_note-102) It gathered strength from the [postmillennial](/wiki/Postmillennial "Postmillennial") belief that the [Second Coming](/wiki/Second_Coming "Second Coming") of Christ would occur after mankind had reformed the entire earth. It was affiliated with the [Social Gospel](/wiki/Social_Gospel "Social Gospel") Movement, which applied Christianity to social issues and gained its force from the Awakening, as did the worldwide missionary movement. New groupings emerged, such as the [Holiness](/wiki/Holiness_movement "Holiness movement"), [Nazarene](/wiki/Church_of_the_Nazarene "Church of the Nazarene"), and [Christian Science](/wiki/Christian_Science "Christian Science") movements.[[92]](#cite_note-Fogel-103)

The [Fourth Great Awakening](/wiki/Fourth_Great_Awakening "Fourth Great Awakening") was a Christian religious awakening that some scholars—most notably, [Robert Fogel](/wiki/Robert_Fogel "Robert Fogel")—say took place in the United States in the late 1960s and early 1970s, while others look at the era following [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The terminology is controversial. Thus, the idea of a Fourth Great Awakening itself has not been generally accepted.[[93]](#cite_note-104)

In 1814, [Le Réveil](/wiki/R%C3%A9veil "Réveil") swept through Calvinist regions in Switzerland and France.

In 1904, a [Protestant revival in Wales](/wiki/1904%E2%80%931905_Welsh_revival "1904–1905 Welsh revival") had a tremendous impact on the local population. A part of British modernization, it drew many people to churches, especially Methodist and Baptist ones.[[94]](#cite_note-105)

A noteworthy development in 20th-century Protestant Christianity was the rise of the modern [Pentecostal movement](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism"). Sprung from Methodist and [Wesleyan](/wiki/John_Wesley "John Wesley") roots, it arose out of meetings at an urban mission on [Azusa Street](/wiki/Azusa_Street "Azusa Street") in Los Angeles. From there it spread around the world, carried by those who experienced what they believed to be miraculous moves of God there. These Pentecost-like manifestations have steadily been in evidence throughout history, such as seen in the two Great Awakenings. Pentecostalism, which in turn birthed the [Charismatic movement](/wiki/Charismatic_movement "Charismatic movement") within already established denominations, continues to be an important force in [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity").

In the United States and elsewhere in the world, there has been a marked rise in the [evangelical wing](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism") of Protestant denominations, especially those that are more exclusively evangelical, and a corresponding decline in the [mainstream liberal churches](/wiki/Mainline_Protestant "Mainline Protestant"). In the post–[World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") era, [Liberal Christianity](/wiki/Liberal_Christianity "Liberal Christianity") was on the rise, and a considerable number of seminaries held and taught from a liberal perspective as well. In the post–[World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") era, the trend began to swing back towards the conservative camp in America's seminaries and church structures.

In Europe, there has been a general move away from religious observance and belief in Christian teachings and a move towards [secularism](/wiki/Secularism "Secularism"). The [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") is largely responsible for the spread of secularism. Some scholars debate the link between Protestantism and the rise of secularism, and take as argument the wide-ranging freedom in Protestant-majority countries.[[95]](#cite_note-106) However, the sole example of France demonstrates that even in Catholic-majority countries, the overwhelming impact of the Enlightenment has brought even stronger secularism and freedom of thought five centuries later. It is more reliable to consider that the Reformation influenced the critical thinkers of the subsequent centuries. Initial philosophers of the Enlightenment were defending a Christian conception of the world, but it was developed together with a fierce and decisive criticism of the Church, its politics, its ethics, its worldview, its scientific and cultural assumptions, leading to the devaluation of all forms of institutionalized Christianity, which extended over the centuries.[[96]](#cite_note-107)

## Radical Reformation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Radical Reformation")]

Main article: [Radical Reformation](/wiki/Radical_Reformation "Radical Reformation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/T%C3%A4uferdisputation_1525.jpg/330px-T%C3%A4uferdisputation_1525.jpg)](/wiki/File:T%C3%A4uferdisputation_1525.jpg)

Dissatisfaction with the outcome of a disputation in 1525 prompted [Swiss Brethren](/wiki/Swiss_Brethren "Swiss Brethren") to part ways with [Huldrych Zwingli](/wiki/Huldrych_Zwingli "Huldrych Zwingli").

Unlike mainstream [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinist "Calvinist") and Zwinglian movements, the [Radical Reformation](/wiki/Radical_Reformation "Radical Reformation"), which had no state sponsorship, generally abandoned the idea of the "Church visible" as distinct from the "Church invisible". It was a rational extension of the state-approved Protestant dissent, which took the value of independence from constituted authority a step further, arguing the same for the civic realm. The Radical Reformation was non-mainstream, though in parts of Germany, Switzerland and Austria, a majority would sympathize with the Radical Reformation despite the intense persecution it faced from both Catholics and Magisterial Protestants.[[97]](#cite_note-horsch-108)

The early [Anabaptists](/wiki/Anabaptists "Anabaptists") believed that their reformation must purify not only theology but also the actual lives of Christians, especially their political and social relationships.[[98]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-109) Therefore, the church should not be supported by the state, neither by tithes and taxes, nor by the use of the sword; [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") was a matter of individual conviction, which could not be forced on anyone, but rather required a personal decision for it.[[98]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-109) Protestant ecclesial leaders such as [Hubmaier](/wiki/Balthasar_Hubmaier "Balthasar Hubmaier") and [Hofmann](/wiki/Melchior_Hoffman "Melchior Hoffman") preached the invalidity of infant baptism, advocating baptism as following conversion (["believer's baptism"](/wiki/Believers_baptism "Believers baptism")) instead. This was not a doctrine new to the reformers, but was taught by earlier groups, such as the [Albigenses](/wiki/Catharism "Catharism") in 1147. Though most of the Radical Reformers were Anabaptist, some did not identify themselves with the mainstream Anabaptist tradition. [Thomas Müntzer](/wiki/Thomas_M%C3%BCntzer "Thomas Müntzer") was involved in the [German Peasants' War](/wiki/German_Peasants%27_War "German Peasants' War"). [Andreas Karlstadt](/wiki/Andreas_Karlstadt "Andreas Karlstadt") disagreed theologically with Huldrych Zwingli and Martin Luther, teaching nonviolence and refusing to baptize infants while not rebaptizing adult believers.[[99]](#cite_note-GAMEO-Karlstadt-110) [Kaspar Schwenkfeld](/wiki/Kaspar_Schwenkfeld "Kaspar Schwenkfeld") and [Sebastian Franck](/wiki/Sebastian_Franck "Sebastian Franck") were influenced by [German mysticism](/wiki/German_mysticism "German mysticism") and [spiritualism](/wiki/Spiritualism_(movement) "Spiritualism (movement)").

In the view of many associated with the Radical Reformation, the [Magisterial Reformation](/wiki/Magisterial_Reformation "Magisterial Reformation") had not gone far enough. Radical Reformer, [Andreas von Bodenstein Karlstadt](/wiki/Andreas_Karlstadt "Andreas Karlstadt"), for example, referred to the Lutheran theologians at [Wittenberg](/wiki/Wittenberg "Wittenberg") as the "new papists".[[100]](#cite_note-111) Since the term "magister" also means "teacher", the Magisterial Reformation is also characterized by an emphasis on the authority of a teacher. This is made evident in the prominence of Luther, Calvin, and Zwingli as leaders of the reform movements in their respective areas of ministry. Because of their authority, they were often criticized by Radical Reformers as being too much like the Roman Popes. A more political side of the Radical Reformation can be seen in the thought and practice of [Hans Hut](/wiki/Hans_Hut "Hans Hut"), although typically Anabaptism has been associated with pacifism.

Anabaptism in shape of its various diversification such as the [Amish](/wiki/Amish "Amish"), [Mennonites](/wiki/Mennonites "Mennonites") and [Hutterites](/wiki/Hutterites "Hutterites") came out of the Radical Reformation. Later in history, [Schwarzenau Brethren](/wiki/Schwarzenau_Brethren "Schwarzenau Brethren"), and the [Apostolic Christian Church](/wiki/Apostolic_Christian_Church "Apostolic Christian Church") would emerge in Anabaptist circles.

## Denominations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Denominations")]

See also: [List of Christian denominations § Protestant](/wiki/List_of_Christian_denominations#Protestant "List of Christian denominations"), and [List of the largest Protestant churches](/wiki/List_of_the_largest_Protestant_churches "List of the largest Protestant churches")

Protestants refer to specific groupings of congregations or churches that share in common foundational doctrines and the name of their groups as [denominations](/wiki/List_of_Christian_Denominations#Protestant "List of Christian Denominations").[[101]](#cite_note-112) The term denomination (national body) is to be distinguished from branch (denominational family; tradition), communion (international body) and congregation (church). An example (this is no universal way to classify Protestant churches, as these may sometimes vary broadly in their structures) to show the difference:

:   Branch/denominational family/tradition: [Methodism](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism")

    :   Communion/international body: [World Methodist Council](/wiki/World_Methodist_Council "World Methodist Council")

        :   Denomination/national body: [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church")

            :   Congregation/church: [First United Methodist Church (Paintsville, Kentucky)](/wiki/First_United_Methodist_Church_(Paintsville,_Kentucky) "First United Methodist Church (Paintsville, Kentucky)")

Strictly and historically speaking however, Protestantism does not include any branch/denomination of Christianity that did not emerge during the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"). This strict definition considers denominations, particularly Non-Denominationalism to be part of a "Post-Protestant/Post-Denominational" culture, or simply an unrelated offshoot of Protestantism.[[102]](#cite_note-113)[[103]](#cite_note-114)[[104]](#cite_note-115) This is due to a multitude of reasons such as the Protestant Reformation ending centuries before many of the offshoot denominations or unaffiliated churches found today (including [Pentecostalism](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism"), [Seventh-day Adventism](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventism "Seventh-day Adventism"), [non-denominational Christianity](/wiki/Non-denominational_Christianity "Non-denominational Christianity"), and more). These branches of Christianity do not share historical heritage to the beginnings of Protestantism.[[105]](#cite_note-116) Furthermore, they differ on multiple key points of theology of historical/confessional Protestant denominations (such as the importance and efficacy of the sacraments), and denominations which came after the Protestant Reformation (most of which are closer to [Restorationism](/wiki/Restorationism "Restorationism")) lack the official creeds and confessions that defined the doctrines of the historic Protestant denominations.[[106]](#cite_note-117)[[107]](#cite_note-118)[[108]](#cite_note-119)[[109]](#cite_note-120) This stricter definition of Protestantism includes only branches such as [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), and [Calvinist/Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_Christianity "Reformed Christianity"), the latter of which includes denominations such as [Presbyterianism](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") and [Continental Reformed](/wiki/Continental_Reformed "Continental Reformed"), [Methodism](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"), [Congregationalism](/wiki/Congregationalism "Congregationalism"), and [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist").[[110]](#cite_note-121)

Protestants reject the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church")'s doctrine that it is the [one true church](/wiki/One_true_church "One true church"), with some teaching belief in the *invisible church*, which consists of all who profess faith in Jesus Christ.[[111]](#cite_note-122) The [Lutheran Church](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") traditionally sees itself as the "main trunk of the historical Christian Tree" founded by Christ and the Apostles, holding that during the Reformation, the [Church of Rome](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") fell away.[[10]](#cite_note-Remensnyder1893-16)[[11]](#cite_note-Frey1918-17) Individual denominations also have formed over very subtle theological differences. Other denominations are simply regional or ethnic expressions of the same beliefs. Because the five solas are the main tenets of the Protestant faith, [non-denominational](/wiki/Nondenominational_Christianity "Nondenominational Christianity") groups and organizations are also considered Protestant.

Various [ecumenical movements](/wiki/Christian_ecumenism "Christian ecumenism") have attempted cooperation or reorganization of the various divided Protestant denominations, according to various models of union, but divisions continue to outpace unions, as there is no overarching authority to which any of the churches owe allegiance, which can authoritatively define the faith. Most denominations share common beliefs in the major aspects of the Christian faith while differing in many secondary doctrines, although what is major and what is secondary is a matter of idiosyncratic belief.

Several countries have [established](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") their [national churches](/wiki/National_church "National church"), linking the ecclesiastical structure with the state. Jurisdictions where a Protestant denomination has been established as a state religion include several [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries"); Denmark (including Greenland),[[112]](#cite_note-DenmarkConstitution-123) [the Faroe Islands](/wiki/The_Faroe_Islands "The Faroe Islands") ([its church](/wiki/Church_of_the_Faroe_Islands "Church of the Faroe Islands") being independent since 2007),[[113]](#cite_note-124) Iceland[[114]](#cite_note-IcelandConstitution-125) and Norway[[115]](#cite_note-abcnyheter-126)[[116]](#cite_note-127)[[117]](#cite_note-128) have established [Evangelical Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") churches. [Tuvalu](/wiki/Tuvalu "Tuvalu") has [the only established church in Reformed tradition](/wiki/Church_of_Tuvalu "Church of Tuvalu") in the world, while [Tonga](/wiki/Tonga "Tonga")—[in the Methodist tradition](/wiki/Free_Wesleyan_Church_of_Tonga "Free Wesleyan Church of Tonga").[[118]](#cite_note-129)

The [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") is the officially established religious institution in England,[[119]](#cite_note-Eberle-130)[[120]](#cite_note-Fox-131)[[121]](#cite_note-Ferrante-132) and also the [Mother Church](/wiki/Mother_Church "Mother Church") of the worldwide [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion").

In 1869, Finland was the first Nordic country to [disestablish](/wiki/Disestablishment "Disestablishment") its [Evangelical Lutheran church](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_of_Finland "Evangelical Lutheran Church of Finland") by introducing the Church Act.[[l]](#cite_note-133) In 2000, Sweden was the second Nordic country to do so.[[122]](#cite_note-134)

### United and uniting churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: United and uniting churches")]

Main article: [United and uniting churches](/wiki/United_and_uniting_churches "United and uniting churches")

See also: [Continuing churches](/wiki/Continuing_churches "Continuing churches")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Union_luthercalvin.jpg/330px-Union_luthercalvin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Union_luthercalvin.jpg)
