Glass window in the town church of [Wiesloch](/wiki/Wiesloch "Wiesloch") featuring [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") and [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin") commemorating the 1821 union of [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") and [Reformed](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") churches in the [Grand Duchy of Baden](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Baden "Grand Duchy of Baden")

United and uniting churches are churches formed from the merger or other form of union of two or more different Protestant denominations.

Historically, unions of Protestant churches were enforced by the state, usually in order to have a stricter control over the religious sphere of its people, but also for other organizational reasons. As modern [Christian ecumenism](/wiki/Christian_ecumenism "Christian ecumenism") progresses, unions between various Protestant traditions are becoming more and more common, resulting in a growing number of united and uniting churches. Some of the recent major examples are the [Church of North India](/wiki/Church_of_North_India "Church of North India") (1970), [United Protestant Church of France](/wiki/United_Protestant_Church_of_France "United Protestant Church of France") (2013), and the [Protestant Church in the Netherlands](/wiki/Protestant_Church_in_the_Netherlands "Protestant Church in the Netherlands") (2004). As mainline Protestantism shrinks in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") due to the rise of [secularism](/wiki/Secularism "Secularism") or in areas where Christianity is a minority religion as with the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"), [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_church "Reformed church"), [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican"), and [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") denominations merge, often creating large nationwide denominations.

What is perhaps the oldest official united church is found in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), where the [Protestant Church in Germany](/wiki/Protestant_Church_in_Germany "Protestant Church in Germany") is a federation of [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), United ([Prussian Union](/wiki/Prussian_Union_of_churches "Prussian Union of churches")), and [Reformed churches](/wiki/Reformed_churches "Reformed churches"), dating back to 1817.[[123]](#cite_note-135)

Around the world, each united or uniting church comprises a different mix of predecessor Protestant denominations. Trends are visible, however, as most united and uniting churches have one or more predecessors with heritage in the [Reformed tradition](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") and many are members of the [World Alliance of Reformed Churches](/wiki/World_Alliance_of_Reformed_Churches "World Alliance of Reformed Churches").

## Major branches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Major branches")]

Protestants can be differentiated according to how they have been influenced by important movements since the Reformation, today regarded as branches. Some of these movements have a common lineage, sometimes directly spawning individual denominations. Due to the earlier stated multitude of [denominations](/wiki/List_of_Christian_denominations "List of Christian denominations"), this section discusses only the largest denominational families, or branches, widely considered to be a part of Protestantism. These are: [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran"), [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican"), [Calvinism (Reformed Christianity)](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism"), [Methodism](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist"), [Hussitism](/wiki/Hussite "Hussite"), [Adventism](/wiki/Adventism "Adventism"), [Pentecostalism](/wiki/Pentecostal "Pentecostal"), [Quakerism](/wiki/Quaker "Quaker"), [Plymouth Brethren](/wiki/Plymouth_Brethren "Plymouth Brethren") and [Baptists](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist"). The small but historically significant branch of [Anabaptism](/wiki/Anabaptist "Anabaptist") is also discussed.

The chart below shows the mutual relations and historical origins of the main Protestant denominational families, or their parts. Due to factors such as [Counterreformation ("Catholic Reformation")](/wiki/Counter-Reformation#Politics "Counter-Reformation") and the legal principle of *[Cuius regio, eius religio](/wiki/Cuius_regio,_eius_religio "Cuius regio, eius religio")*, many people lived as [Nicodemites](/wiki/Nicodemite "Nicodemite"), where their professed religious affiliations were more or less at odds with the movement they sympathized with. As a result, the boundaries between the denominations do not separate as cleanly as this chart indicates. When a population was suppressed or persecuted into feigning an adherence to the dominant faith, over the generations they continued to influence the church they outwardly adhered to.

Because Calvinism was not specifically recognized in the Holy Roman Empire until the 1648 Peace of Westphalia, many Calvinists lived as [Crypto-Calvinists](/wiki/Crypto-Calvinists "Crypto-Calvinists"). Due to Counterreformation ("Catholic Reformation") related suppressions in Catholic lands during the 16th through 19th centuries, many Protestants lived as [Crypto-Protestants](/wiki/Crypto-protestantism "Crypto-protestantism"). Meanwhile, in Protestant areas, Catholics sometimes lived as [crypto-papists](/wiki/Crypto-papist "Crypto-papist"), although in continental Europe emigration was more feasible so this was less common.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Protestant_branches.svg/960px-Protestant_branches.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Protestant_branches.svg)

Historical chart of the main branches of Protestantism

### Lutheranism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Lutheranism")]

Main article: [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/LutherRose.jpg/250px-LutherRose.jpg)](/wiki/File:LutherRose.jpg)

[Luther's rose seal](/wiki/Luther_rose "Luther rose"), a symbol of [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism")

[Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") identifies with the [theology of Martin Luther](/wiki/Theology_of_Martin_Luther "Theology of Martin Luther"). It advocates a doctrine of justification "by [grace alone](/wiki/Sola_gratia "Sola gratia") through [faith alone](/wiki/Sola_fide "Sola fide") on the basis of [Scripture alone](/wiki/Sola_scriptura "Sola scriptura")", the doctrine that scripture is the final authority on all matters of faith, rejecting the assertion made by Catholic leaders at the [Council of Trent](/wiki/Council_of_Trent "Council of Trent") that authority comes from both Scriptures and [Tradition](/wiki/Sacred_tradition "Sacred tradition").[[124]](#cite_note-136) In addition, Lutherans accept the teachings of the first four [ecumenical councils](/wiki/Ecumenical_councils "Ecumenical councils") of the undivided Christian Church.[[125]](#cite_note-Olson1999-137)[[126]](#cite_note-Kelly2009-138)

Unlike the Reformed tradition, Lutherans retain many of the [liturgical](/wiki/Christian_liturgy "Christian liturgy") practices and [sacramental](/wiki/Sacraments#Lutheran_teaching "Sacraments") teachings of the pre-Reformation Church with an emphasis on the [Eucharist](/wiki/Sacramental_union "Sacramental union"), or Lord's Supper. Lutheran theology differs from Reformed theology in [Christology](/wiki/Scholastic_Lutheran_Christology "Scholastic Lutheran Christology"), the purpose of [God's Law](/wiki/Law_and_Gospel#Lutheran_and_Reformed_differences "Law and Gospel"), divine [grace](/wiki/Irresistible_grace#Lutheran "Irresistible grace"), the concept of [perseverance of the saints](/wiki/Perseverance_of_the_saints#Lutheran_view "Perseverance of the saints"), and [predestination](/wiki/Predestination#Lutheranism "Predestination").

Today, Lutheranism is one of the largest branches of Protestantism. With approximately 80 million adherents,[[127]](#cite_note-139) it constitutes the third most common Protestant confession after historically [Pentecostal](/wiki/Pentecostal "Pentecostal") denominations and [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism").[[14]](#cite_note-pewforum1-22) The [Lutheran World Federation](/wiki/Lutheran_World_Federation "Lutheran World Federation"), the largest global communion of Lutheran churches represents over 72 million people.[[128]](#cite_note-140) Both of these figures miscount Lutherans worldwide as many members of more generically Protestant LWF member church bodies do not self-identify as Lutheran or attend congregations that self-identify as Lutheran.[[129]](#cite_note-141) Additionally, there are other international organizations such as the [Global Confessional and Missional Lutheran Forum](/wiki/Global_Confessional_and_Missional_Lutheran_Forum "Global Confessional and Missional Lutheran Forum"), [International Lutheran Council](/wiki/International_Lutheran_Council "International Lutheran Council") and the [Confessional Evangelical Lutheran Conference](/wiki/Confessional_Evangelical_Lutheran_Conference "Confessional Evangelical Lutheran Conference"), as well as [Lutheran denominations](/wiki/List_of_Lutheran_denominations "List of Lutheran denominations") that are not necessarily a member of an international organization.

### Anglicanism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Anglicanism")]

Main article: [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Thomas_Cranmer_by_Gerlach_Flicke.jpg/250px-Thomas_Cranmer_by_Gerlach_Flicke.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Cranmer_by_Gerlach_Flicke.jpg)

[Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer"), one of the most influential figures in shaping Anglican theology and self-identity

[Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") consists of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") and churches which are historically tied to it or hold similar beliefs, worship practices and church structures.[[130]](#cite_note-cofe-142) The word *Anglican* originates in **ecclesia anglicana**, a [medieval Latin](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin") phrase dating to at least 1246 that means the *English Church*. There is no single "Anglican Church" with universal juridical authority, since each national or regional church has full [autonomy](/wiki/Autonomy "Autonomy"). As the name suggests, the communion is an association of churches in [full communion](/wiki/Full_communion "Full communion") with the [archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury"). The great majority of Anglicans are members of churches which are part of the international [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"),[[131]](#cite_note-acomm-143) which has 85 million adherents.[[132]](#cite_note-144)

The [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") declared its independence from the Catholic Church at the time of the [Elizabethan Religious Settlement](/wiki/Elizabethan_Religious_Settlement "Elizabethan Religious Settlement").[[133]](#cite_note-CTS-145) Many of the new Anglican formularies of the mid-16th century corresponded closely to those of contemporary Reformed tradition. These reforms were understood by one of those most responsible for them, the then archbishop of Canterbury, [Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer"), as navigating a middle way between two of the emerging Protestant traditions, namely Lutheranism and Calvinism.[[134]](#cite_note-146)

Unique to Anglicanism is the *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")*, the collection of services that worshippers in most Anglican churches used for centuries. While it has since undergone many revisions and Anglican churches in different countries have developed other service books, the Book of Common Prayer is still acknowledged as one of the ties that bind the Anglican Communion together. The [Thirty-Nine Articles](/wiki/Thirty-Nine_Articles "Thirty-Nine Articles") and the [Books of Homilies](/wiki/Books_of_Homilies "Books of Homilies") explicate historic Anglican doctrine and along with the Book of Common Prayer, were developed under the reformer Thomas Cranmer.[[135]](#cite_note-Samuel2020-147)

### Calvinism (Reformed Christianity)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Calvinism (Reformed Christianity)")]

Main article: [Reformed Christianity](/wiki/Reformed_Christianity "Reformed Christianity")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/John_Calvin_-_Young.jpg/250px-John_Calvin_-_Young.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Calvin_-_Young.jpg)

[John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin")'s theological thought influenced a variety of [Congregational](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church"), [Continental Reformed](/wiki/Continental_Reformed_church "Continental Reformed church"), [United](/wiki/United_and_uniting_churches "United and uniting churches"), [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism"), and other Reformed churches.

[Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism"), also called the Reformed tradition, was advanced by several theologians such as [Martin Bucer](/wiki/Martin_Bucer "Martin Bucer"), [Heinrich Bullinger](/wiki/Heinrich_Bullinger "Heinrich Bullinger"), [Peter Martyr Vermigli](/wiki/Peter_Martyr_Vermigli "Peter Martyr Vermigli"), and Huldrych Zwingli, but this branch of Christianity bears the name of the French reformer John Calvin because of his prominent influence on it and because of his role in the confessional and ecclesiastical debates throughout the 16th century.

This term also refers to the doctrines and practices of the [Reformed churches](/wiki/Reformed_churches "Reformed churches") of which Calvin was an early leader. Less commonly, it can refer to the individual teaching of Calvin himself. The particulars of Calvinist theology may be stated in a number of ways. Perhaps the best known summary is contained in the [five points of Calvinism](/wiki/Five_points_of_Calvinism "Five points of Calvinism"), though these points identify the Calvinist view on [soteriology](/wiki/Christian_soteriology "Christian soteriology") rather than summarizing the system as a whole. Broadly speaking, Calvinism stresses the sovereignty or rule of God in all things—in salvation but also in all of life. This concept is seen clearly in the doctrines of [predestination](/wiki/Predestination_(Calvinism) "Predestination (Calvinism)") and [total depravity](/wiki/Total_depravity "Total depravity").

The biggest Reformed association is the [World Communion of Reformed Churches](/wiki/World_Communion_of_Reformed_Churches "World Communion of Reformed Churches") with more than 80 million members in 211 member denominations around the world.[[136]](#cite_note-148)[[137]](#cite_note-149) There are more conservative Reformed federations like the [World Reformed Fellowship](/wiki/World_Reformed_Fellowship "World Reformed Fellowship") and the [International Conference of Reformed Churches](/wiki/International_Conference_of_Reformed_Churches "International Conference of Reformed Churches"), as well as [independent churches](/wiki/List_of_Reformed_denominations "List of Reformed denominations").

### Methodism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Methodism")]

Main article: [Methodism](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/The_history_of_Methodism_%281902%29_%2814742412186%29.jpg/250px-The_history_of_Methodism_%281902%29_%2814742412186%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_history_of_Methodism_(1902)_(14742412186).jpg)

[John Wesley](/wiki/John_Wesley "John Wesley"), founder of Methodism, [preaching in the open air](/wiki/Open-air_preaching "Open-air preaching")

[Methodism](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") identifies principally with [the theology](/wiki/Wesleyan_theology "Wesleyan theology") of [John Wesley](/wiki/John_Wesley "John Wesley"). This evangelical movement originated as a [revival](/wiki/Christian_revival "Christian revival") within the 18th-century [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") and became a separate Church following Wesley's death. Because of vigorous missionary activity, the movement spread throughout the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), the United States, and beyond, today claiming approximately 80 million adherents worldwide.[[138]](#cite_note-150) Originally it appealed especially to laborers and slaves.

[Soteriologically](/wiki/Soteriology "Soteriology"), most Methodists are [Arminian](/wiki/Arminian "Arminian"), emphasizing that Christ accomplished salvation for every human being, and that humans must exercise an act of the will to receive it (as opposed to the traditional Calvinist doctrine of [monergism](/wiki/Monergism "Monergism")). Methodism is traditionally [low church](/wiki/Low_church "Low church") in liturgy, although this varies greatly between individual congregations; the Wesleys themselves greatly valued the Anglican liturgy and tradition. Methodism is known for its rich musical tradition; John Wesley's brother, [Charles](/wiki/Charles_Wesley "Charles Wesley"), was instrumental in writing much of the [hymnody](/wiki/Hymnody "Hymnody") of the Methodist Church,[[139]](#cite_note-Methodist_Hymnbody-151) and many other eminent hymn writers come from the Methodist tradition.
The Holiness movement refers to a set of practices surrounding the doctrine of Christian perfection that emerged within 19th-century Methodism, along with a number of evangelical denominations and [parachurch organizations](/wiki/Parachurch_organization "Parachurch organization") (such as [camp meetings](/wiki/Camp_meeting "Camp meeting")).[[140]](#cite_note-Winn-152) There are an estimated 12 million adherents in denominations aligned with the Wesleyan-holiness movement.[[141]](#cite_note-153) The [Free Methodist Church](/wiki/Free_Methodist_Church "Free Methodist Church"), the [Salvation Army](/wiki/Salvation_Army "Salvation Army") and the [Wesleyan Methodist Church](/wiki/Wesleyan_Church "Wesleyan Church") are notable examples, while other adherents of the Holiness Movement remained within mainline Methodism, e.g. the [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church").[[140]](#cite_note-Winn-152)

### Hussitism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Hussitism")]

[Hussitism](/wiki/Hussitism "Hussitism") follows the teachings of Czech reformer Jan Hus, who became the best-known representative of the [Bohemian Reformation](/wiki/Bohemian_Reformation "Bohemian Reformation") and one of the forerunners of the Protestant Reformation. An early hymnal was the hand-written *[Jistebnice hymn book](/wiki/Jistebnice_hymn_book "Jistebnice hymn book")*. This predominantly religious movement was propelled by social issues and strengthened [Czech](/wiki/Czech_people "Czech people") national awareness. Among present-day Christians, Hussite traditions are represented in the [Moravian Church](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"), [Unity of the Brethren](/wiki/Unity_of_the_Brethren_(Texas) "Unity of the Brethren (Texas)") and the [Czechoslovak Hussite Church](/wiki/Czechoslovak_Hussite_Church "Czechoslovak Hussite Church").[[142]](#cite_note-Nĕmec-154)

### Adventism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Adventism")]

Main article: [Adventism](/wiki/Adventism "Adventism")

Adventism began in the 19th century in the context of the [Second Great Awakening](/wiki/Second_Great_Awakening "Second Great Awakening") revival in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). The name refers to belief in the imminent [Second Coming of Christ](/wiki/Second_Coming_of_Christ "Second Coming of Christ"). [William Miller](/wiki/William_Miller_(preacher) "William Miller (preacher)") started the Adventist movement in the 1830s. His followers became known as [Millerites](/wiki/Millerism "Millerism").[[143]](#cite_note-Bergman-155)

Although the Adventist churches hold much in common, their [theologies](/wiki/Christian_theology "Christian theology") differ on whether the [intermediate state](/wiki/Intermediate_state_(Christianity) "Intermediate state (Christianity)") is [unconscious sleep](/wiki/Soul_sleep "Soul sleep") or consciousness, whether the ultimate punishment of the wicked is [annihilation](/wiki/Annihilationism "Annihilationism") or eternal torment, the nature of immortality, whether or not the wicked are resurrected after the millennium, and whether the sanctuary of [Daniel 8](/wiki/Daniel_8 "Daniel 8") refers to the one in [heaven](/wiki/Heavenly_sanctuary "Heavenly sanctuary") or one on earth.[[144]](#cite_note-Handbook-156) The movement has encouraged the examination of the whole [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible"), leading Seventh-day Adventists and some smaller Adventist groups to observe the [Sabbath](/wiki/Sabbath_in_Seventh-day_Adventism "Sabbath in Seventh-day Adventism"). The [General Conference of Seventh-day Adventists](/wiki/General_Conference_of_Seventh-day_Adventists "General Conference of Seventh-day Adventists") has compiled that church's core beliefs in [the 28 Fundamental Beliefs](/wiki/The_28_Fundamental_Beliefs "The 28 Fundamental Beliefs") (1980 and 2005), which use Biblical references as justification.

In 2010, Adventism claimed some 22 million believers scattered in various independent churches.[[14]](#cite_note-pewforum1-22) The largest church within the movement—the [Seventh-day Adventist Church](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church")—has more than 18 million members.

### Pentecostalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Pentecostalism")]

Main article: [Pentecostalism](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/cf/Charlesparham.png)](/wiki/File:Charlesparham.png)

[Charles Fox Parham](/wiki/Charles_Fox_Parham "Charles Fox Parham"), who associated [glossolalia](/wiki/Speaking_in_tongues "Speaking in tongues") with the baptism in the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity")

[Pentecostalism](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism") is a movement that places special emphasis on a direct personal experience of [God](/wiki/God_in_Christianity "God in Christianity") through the [baptism with the Holy Spirit](/wiki/Baptism_with_the_Holy_Spirit "Baptism with the Holy Spirit"). The term *Pentecostal* is derived from [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost"), the [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") name for the Jewish [Feast of Weeks](/wiki/Feast_of_Weeks "Feast of Weeks"). For Christians, this event commemorates the descent of the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_(Christianity) "Holy Spirit (Christianity)") upon the followers of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ"), as described in the [second chapter](/wiki/Second_Chapter_of_Acts "Second Chapter of Acts") of the [Book of Acts](/wiki/Book_of_Acts "Book of Acts").

This branch of Protestantism is distinguished by belief in the baptism with the Holy Spirit as an experience separate from [conversion](/wiki/Conversion_to_Christianity "Conversion to Christianity") that enables a Christian to live a life empowered by and filled with the Holy Spirit. This empowerment includes the use of [spiritual gifts](/wiki/Spiritual_gift "Spiritual gift") such as [speaking in tongues](/wiki/Speaking_in_tongues "Speaking in tongues") and [divine healing](/wiki/Divine_healing "Divine healing")—two other defining characteristics of Pentecostalism. Because of their commitment to biblical authority, spiritual gifts, and the miraculous, Pentecostals tend to see their movement as reflecting the same kind of spiritual power and teachings that were found in the [Apostolic Age](/wiki/Apostolic_Age "Apostolic Age") of the [early church](/wiki/Early_church "Early church"). For this reason, some Pentecostals also use the term *Apostolic* or *[Full Gospel](/wiki/Full_Gospel "Full Gospel")* to describe their movement.

Pentecostalism eventually spawned hundreds of new denominations, including large groups such as the Assemblies of God and the Church of God in Christ, both in the United States and elsewhere. There are over 279 million Pentecostals worldwide, and the movement is growing in many parts of the world, especially the [global South](/wiki/Global_South "Global South"). Since the 1960s, Pentecostalism has increasingly gained acceptance from other Christian traditions, and Pentecostal beliefs concerning Spirit baptism and spiritual gifts have been embraced by non-Pentecostal Christians in Protestant and [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") churches through the [Charismatic Movement](/wiki/Charismatic_Movement "Charismatic Movement"). Together, [Pentecostal and Charismatic Christianity](/wiki/Charismatic_Christianity "Charismatic Christianity") numbers over 500 million adherents.[[145]](#cite_note-PewGlobalChristianity67-157)

### Quakerism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Quakerism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Fox_by_Lely_2.jpg/250px-Fox_by_Lely_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fox_by_Lely_2.jpg)

[George Fox](/wiki/George_Fox "George Fox") was an [English dissenter](/wiki/English_dissenter "English dissenter") and a founder of the [Religious Society of Friends](/wiki/Religious_Society_of_Friends "Religious Society of Friends"), commonly known as the Quakers or Friends.

[Quakers](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers"), or Friends, are members of a family of religious movements collectively known as the Religious Society of Friends. The central unifying doctrine of these movements is the [priesthood of all believers](/wiki/Priesthood_of_all_believers "Priesthood of all believers").[[146]](#cite_note-158)[[147]](#cite_note-159) Many Friends view themselves as members of a Christian denomination. They include those with [evangelical](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism"), [holiness](/wiki/Holiness_movement "Holiness movement"), [liberal](/wiki/Mainline_Protestant "Mainline Protestant"), and traditional [conservative Quaker](/wiki/Conservative_Friends "Conservative Friends") understandings of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"). Unlike many other groups that emerged within Christianity, the Religious Society of Friends has actively tried to avoid [creeds](/wiki/Creed "Creed") and hierarchical structures.[[148]](#cite_note-160)

### Plymouth Brethren

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Plymouth Brethren")]

The [Plymouth Brethren](/wiki/Plymouth_Brethren "Plymouth Brethren") are a [conservative](/wiki/Conservative "Conservative"), low church, evangelical denomination, whose history can be traced to [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin"), Ireland, in the late 1820s, originating from [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism").[[149]](#cite_note-161)[[150]](#cite_note-mackay1981-162) Among other beliefs, the group emphasizes **sola scriptura**. Brethren generally see themselves not as a denomination, but as a network, or even as a collection of overlapping networks, of like-minded independent churches. Although the group refused for many years to take any denominational name to itself—a stance that some of them still maintain—the title *The Brethren*, is one that many of their number are comfortable with in that the Bible designates all believers as *brethren*.

### Baptists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Baptists")]

Main article: [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Baptism_by_immersion.jpg/250px-Baptism_by_immersion.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baptism_by_immersion.jpg)

Baptists subscribe to a doctrine that baptism should be performed [only for professing believers](/wiki/Believer%27s_baptism "Believer's baptism").

[Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists") subscribe to a doctrine that baptism should be performed only for professing believers ([believer's baptism](/wiki/Believer%27s_baptism "Believer's baptism"), as opposed to [infant baptism](/wiki/Infant_baptism "Infant baptism")), and that it must be done by complete [immersion](/wiki/Immersion_baptism "Immersion baptism") (as opposed to [affusion](/wiki/Affusion "Affusion") or [sprinkling](/wiki/Aspersion "Aspersion")). Baptists also claim that infant baptisms are false due to the infant's lack of understanding of virtues and sins, making them not able (according to Baptists) to confess their faith, which also regarded their lack of being able to speak at such a young age, compared to adults.[[151]](#cite_note-auto1-163) Other [tenets](/wiki/Dogma "Dogma") of Baptist churches include [soul competency](/wiki/Soul_competency "Soul competency") (liberty), [salvation](/wiki/Salvation "Salvation") through [faith alone](/wiki/Sola_fide "Sola fide"), [Scripture alone](/wiki/Sola_scriptura "Sola scriptura") as the rule of faith and practice, and the autonomy of the local [congregation](/wiki/Congregationalist_polity "Congregationalist polity"). Baptists recognize two ministerial offices, [pastors](/wiki/Pastor "Pastor") and [deacons](/wiki/Deacon "Deacon"). Baptist churches are widely considered to be Protestant churches, though some Baptists disavow this identity.[[152]](#cite_note-Baptist_Origins-164)

Diverse from their beginning, those identifying as Baptists today differ widely from one another in what they believe, how they worship, their attitudes toward other Christians, and their understanding of what is important in Christian discipleship.[[153]](#cite_note-Shurden_turning-165) The separation of Church and state is also a huge belief of Anabaptists. They have strongly supported this because they believe its a Biblical concept to follow, and they were persecuted by Protestant and Catholic authorities. Arguing that Christ did not give magistrates the power to form churches or constrain citizens in matters of religion.[[151]](#cite_note-auto1-163) There is some disagreement regarding the precise origins of the Anabaptists, but majority of scholars claim that the Anabaptist religion began around 1525 in Zurich, Switzerland.[[154]](#cite_note-166)

Historians trace the earliest church labeled *Baptist* back to 1609 in [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam"), with [English Separatist](/wiki/English_Dissenters "English Dissenters") [John Smyth](/wiki/John_Smyth_(Baptist_minister) "John Smyth (Baptist minister)") as its pastor.[[155]](#cite_note-Gourley-167) Baptist practice spread to England, where the General Baptists considered Christ's atonement to extend to all people, while the Particular Baptists believed that it extended only to [the elect](/wiki/Election_(Christianity) "Election (Christianity)"). In 1638, [Roger Williams](/wiki/Roger_Williams "Roger Williams") established the [first Baptist congregation in the North American colonies](/wiki/First_Baptist_church_in_America "First Baptist church in America"). In the mid-18th century, the [First Great Awakening](/wiki/First_Great_Awakening "First Great Awakening") increased Baptist growth in both New England and the South.[[156]](#cite_note-EBOself-168) The [Second Great Awakening](/wiki/Second_Great_Awakening "Second Great Awakening") in the South in the early 19th century increased church membership, as did the preachers' lessening of support for [abolition](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States") and [manumission](/wiki/Manumission "Manumission") of [slavery](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery"), which had been part of the 18th-century teachings. Baptist missionaries have spread their church to every continent.[[157]](#cite_note-ODCC_self-169)

The [Baptist World Alliance](/wiki/Baptist_World_Alliance "Baptist World Alliance") reports more than 41 million members in more than 150,000 congregations.[[158]](#cite_note-170) In 2002, there were over 100 million Baptists and Baptistic group members worldwide and over 33 million in North America.[[157]](#cite_note-ODCC_self-169) The largest Baptist association is the [Southern Baptist Convention](/wiki/Southern_Baptist_Convention "Southern Baptist Convention"), with the membership of associated churches totaling more than 14 million.[[159]](#cite_note-171)

### Anabaptism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Anabaptism")]

Main article: [Anabaptism](/wiki/Anabaptism "Anabaptism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Lancaster_County_Amish_03.jpg/250px-Lancaster_County_Amish_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lancaster_County_Amish_03.jpg)

An [Amish](/wiki/Amish "Amish") family in a horse-drawn square buggy in [Lancaster County, Pennsylvania, United States](/wiki/Lancaster_County,_Pennsylvania "Lancaster County, Pennsylvania")

Anabaptism traces its origins to the [Radical Reformation](/wiki/Radical_Reformation "Radical Reformation"). Anabaptists believe in delaying [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") until the candidate confesses his or her faith. Although some consider this movement to be an offshoot of Protestantism, others see it as a distinct one.[[160]](#cite_note-172)[[161]](#cite_note-173) The [Amish](/wiki/Amish "Amish"), [Hutterites](/wiki/Hutterites "Hutterites"), and [Mennonites](/wiki/Mennonites "Mennonites") are direct descendants of the movement. [Schwarzenau Brethren](/wiki/Schwarzenau_Brethren "Schwarzenau Brethren"), [Bruderhof](/wiki/Bruderhof "Bruderhof"), and the [Apostolic Christian Church](/wiki/Apostolic_Christian_Church "Apostolic Christian Church") are considered later developments among the Anabaptists.

The name *Anabaptist*, meaning "one who baptizes again", was given to them by their persecutors in reference to the practice of re-baptizing converts who already had been baptized as infants.[[162]](#cite_note-174) Anabaptists required that baptismal candidates be able to make their own confessions of faith and so rejected [baptism of infants](/wiki/Infant_baptism "Infant baptism"). The early members of this movement did not accept the name *Anabaptist*, claiming that since infant baptism was unscriptural and null and void, the baptizing of believers was not a re-baptism but in fact their first real baptism. As a result of their views on the nature of baptism and other issues, Anabaptists were heavily persecuted during the 16th century and into the 17th by both [Magisterial Protestants](/wiki/Magisterial_Reformation "Magisterial Reformation") and Catholics. While most Anabaptists adhered to a [literal interpretation of the Sermon on the Mount](/wiki/Sermon_on_the_Mount#Analysis_and_interpretation "Sermon on the Mount"), which precluded taking oaths, participating in military actions, and participating in civil government, some who practiced re-baptism felt otherwise.[[m]](#cite_note-175) They were thus technically Anabaptists, even though conservative [Amish](/wiki/Amish "Amish"), [Mennonites](/wiki/Mennonites "Mennonites"), and [Hutterites](/wiki/Hutterites "Hutterites") and some historians tend to consider them as outside of true Anabaptism. Anabaptist reformers of the Radical Reformation are divided into Radical and the so-called Second Front. Some important Radical Reformation theologians were [John of Leiden](/wiki/John_of_Leiden "John of Leiden"), [Thomas Müntzer](/wiki/Thomas_M%C3%BCntzer "Thomas Müntzer"), [Kaspar Schwenkfeld](/wiki/Kaspar_Schwenkfeld_von_Ossig "Kaspar Schwenkfeld von Ossig"), [Sebastian Franck](/wiki/Sebastian_Franck "Sebastian Franck"), and [Menno Simons](/wiki/Menno_Simons "Menno Simons"). Second Front Reformers included [Hans Denck](/wiki/Hans_Denck "Hans Denck"), [Conrad Grebel](/wiki/Conrad_Grebel "Conrad Grebel"), [Balthasar Hubmaier](/wiki/Balthasar_Hubmaier "Balthasar Hubmaier") and [Felix Manz](/wiki/Felix_Manz "Felix Manz"). Many Anabaptists today still use the *[Ausbund](/wiki/Ausbund "Ausbund")*, which is the oldest hymnal still in continuous use.

### Other Protestants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Other Protestants")]

Main article: [List of Christian denominations § Protestant](/wiki/List_of_Christian_denominations#Protestant "List of Christian denominations")

There are many other Protestant denominations that do not fit neatly into the mentioned branches, and are far smaller in membership. Some groups of individuals who hold basic Protestant tenets identify themselves simply as "Christians" or "[born-again](/wiki/Born-again "Born-again") Christians". They typically distance themselves from the [confessionalism](/wiki/Confessionalism_(religion) "Confessionalism (religion)") or creedalism of other Christian communities[[163]](#cite_note-176) by calling themselves "[non-denominational](/wiki/Non-denominational_Christianity "Non-denominational Christianity")" or "[evangelical](/wiki/Evangelical "Evangelical")". Often founded by individual pastors, they have little affiliation with historic denominations.[[164]](#cite_note-Pew_Forum_on_Religion_&_Public_Life_/_U.S._Religious_Landscape_Survey-177)

Although [Unitarianism](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism") developed from the Protestant Reformation,[[165]](#cite_note-178) it is excluded from Protestantism due to its [Nontrinitarian](/wiki/Nontrinitarian "Nontrinitarian") theological nature.[[42]](#cite_note-willsky-51)[[166]](#cite_note-179) Unitarianism has been popular in the [region of Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania") within today's [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"), England, and the United States.[[42]](#cite_note-willsky-51) It originated almost simultaneously in Transylvania and the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth").

[Spiritual Christianity](/wiki/Spiritual_Christianity "Spiritual Christianity") is the group of Russian movements ([Doukhobors](/wiki/Doukhobors "Doukhobors") and others), so-called folk Protestants. Their origins are varied: some were influenced by western Protestants, others from disgust of the behavior of official [Orthodox](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church") priests.[[167]](#cite_note-180)[[168]](#cite_note-181)

[Messianic Judaism](/wiki/Messianic_Judaism "Messianic Judaism") is a movement of the Jews and non-Jews, which arose in the 1960s within Evangelical Protestantism and absorbed elements of the [messianic](/wiki/Jewish_Christian "Jewish Christian") traditions in Judaism.[[169]](#cite_note-182)

## Interdenominational movements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Interdenominational movements")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Messiah_Cathedral_in_Night.jpg/330px-Messiah_Cathedral_in_Night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Messiah_Cathedral_in_Night.jpg)

An [Indonesian Reformed Evangelical Church](/wiki/Indonesian_Reformed_Evangelical_Church "Indonesian Reformed Evangelical Church") [megachurch](/wiki/Megachurch "Megachurch")

There are also Christian movements which cross denominational lines and even branches, and cannot be classified on the same level previously mentioned forms. [Evangelicalism](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism") is a prominent example. Some of those movements are active exclusively within Protestantism, some are Christian-wide. Transdenominational movements are sometimes capable of affecting parts of the Catholic Church, such as does it the [Charismatic Movement](/wiki/Charismatic_Movement "Charismatic Movement"), which aims to incorporate beliefs and practices similar to [Pentecostals](/wiki/Pentecostals "Pentecostals") into the various branches of Christianity. [Neo-charismatic churches](/wiki/Neo-charismatic_churches "Neo-charismatic churches") are sometimes regarded as a subgroup of the Charismatic Movement. Both are put under a common label of [Charismatic Christianity](/wiki/Charismatic_Christianity "Charismatic Christianity") (so-called *Renewalists*), along with Pentecostals. [Nondenominational churches](/wiki/Nondenominational_Christianity "Nondenominational Christianity") and various [house churches](/wiki/House_church "House church") often adopt, or are akin to one of these movements.

[Megachurches](/wiki/Megachurch "Megachurch") are usually influenced by interdenominational movements. Globally, these large congregations are a significant development in Protestant Christianity. In the United States, the phenomenon has more than quadrupled in the past two decades.[[170]](#cite_note-183) It has since spread worldwide.

The chart below shows the mutual relations and historical origins of the main interdenominational movements and other developments within Protestantism.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Interdenominational_movements_%26_other_Protestant_developments.svg/960px-Interdenominational_movements_%26_other_Protestant_developments.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Interdenominational_movements_%26_other_Protestant_developments.svg)

Links between interdenominational movements and other developments within Protestantism

### Evangelicalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Evangelicalism")]

Main article: [Evangelicalism](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Bundesarchiv_Bild_194-0798-29%2C_D%C3%BCsseldorf%2C_Veranstaltung_mit_Billy_Graham.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_194-0798-29%2C_D%C3%BCsseldorf%2C_Veranstaltung_mit_Billy_Graham.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_194-0798-29,_D%C3%BCsseldorf,_Veranstaltung_mit_Billy_Graham.jpg)

[Billy Graham](/wiki/Billy_Graham "Billy Graham"), a prominent evangelical revivalist, preaching in [Duisburg, Germany](/wiki/Duisburg "Duisburg"), in 1954

Evangelicalism, or evangelical Protestantism,[[n]](#cite_note-184) is a worldwide, transdenominational movement which maintains that the essence of [the gospel](/wiki/The_gospel "The gospel") consists in the doctrine of salvation by [grace](/wiki/Grace_in_Christianity "Grace in Christianity") through [faith](/wiki/Faith_in_Christianity "Faith in Christianity") in [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus")'s [atonement](/wiki/Atonement_in_Christianity "Atonement in Christianity").[[171]](#cite_note-Oxf-185)[[172]](#cite_note-186)

Evangelicals are [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians") who believe in the centrality of the conversion or ["born again" experience](/wiki/Born_again_(Christianity) "Born again (Christianity)") in receiving salvation, believe in the authority of the Bible as God's revelation to humanity and have a strong commitment to evangelism or sharing the Christian message.

It gained great momentum in the 18th and 19th centuries with the emergence of [Methodism](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") and the [Great Awakenings](/wiki/Great_Awakenings "Great Awakenings") in Britain and North America. The origins of Evangelicalism are usually traced back to the English [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist") movement, [Nicolaus Zinzendorf](/wiki/Nicolaus_Zinzendorf "Nicolaus Zinzendorf"), the [Moravian Church](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"), [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") [Pietism](/wiki/Pietism "Pietism"), [Presbyterianism](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") and [Puritanism](/wiki/Puritanism "Puritanism").[[14]](#cite_note-pewforum1-22) Among leaders and major figures of the Evangelical Protestant movement were [John Wesley](/wiki/John_Wesley "John Wesley"), [George Whitefield](/wiki/George_Whitefield "George Whitefield"), [Jonathan Edwards](/wiki/Jonathan_Edwards_(theologian) "Jonathan Edwards (theologian)"), [Billy Graham](/wiki/Billy_Graham "Billy Graham"), [Harold John Ockenga](/wiki/Harold_John_Ockenga "Harold John Ockenga"), [John Stott](/wiki/John_Stott "John Stott") and [Martyn Lloyd-Jones](/wiki/Martyn_Lloyd-Jones "Martyn Lloyd-Jones").

There are an estimated 285,480,000 Evangelicals, corresponding to 13% of the [Christian population](/wiki/Christianity_by_country "Christianity by country") and 4% of the [total world population](/wiki/World_population "World population"). The Americas, Africa and Asia are home to the majority of Evangelicals. The United States has the largest concentration of Evangelicals.[[173]](#cite_note-How_Many_Evangelicals_Are_There-187) Evangelicalism is gaining popularity, especially in Latin America and the [developing world](/wiki/Developing_world "Developing world").

### Charismatic movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Charismatic movement")]

Main article: [Charismatic movement](/wiki/Charismatic_movement "Charismatic movement")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Hillsong_Church_Konstanz_2018.jpg/330px-Hillsong_Church_Konstanz_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hillsong_Church_Konstanz_2018.jpg)

Hillsong Church, an evangelical charismatic church, in [Konstanz](/wiki/Konstanz "Konstanz"), Germany

The Charismatic movement is the international trend of historically mainstream congregations adopting beliefs and practices similar to [Pentecostals](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism"). Fundamental to the movement is the use of [spiritual gifts](/wiki/Spiritual_gift "Spiritual gift"). Among Protestants, the movement began around 1960.

In the United States, Episcopalian [Dennis Bennett](/wiki/Dennis_Bennett_(priest) "Dennis Bennett (priest)") is sometimes cited as one of the charismatic movement's seminal influence.[[174]](#cite_note-188) In the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), [Colin Urquhart](/wiki/Colin_Urquhart "Colin Urquhart"), [Michael Harper](/wiki/Michael_Harper_(priest) "Michael Harper (priest)"), [David Watson](/wiki/David_Watson_(evangelist) "David Watson (evangelist)") and others were in the vanguard of similar developments. The [Massey](/wiki/Massey_University "Massey University") conference in New Zealand, 1964 was attended by several Anglicans, including the Rev. Ray Muller, who went on to invite Bennett to New Zealand in 1966, and played a leading role in developing and promoting the *Life in the Spirit* seminars. Other Charismatic movement leaders in New Zealand include [Bill Subritzky](/wiki/Bill_Subritzky "Bill Subritzky").

Larry Christenson, a Lutheran theologian based in [San Pedro, California](/wiki/San_Pedro,_California "San Pedro, California"), did much in the 1960s and 1970s to interpret the charismatic movement for Lutherans. A very large annual conference regarding that matter was held in [Minneapolis](/wiki/Minneapolis "Minneapolis"). Charismatic Lutheran congregations in Minnesota became especially large and influential; especially "Hosanna!" in Lakeville, and North Heights in St. Paul. The next generation of Lutheran charismatics cluster around the Alliance of Renewal Churches. There is considerable charismatic activity among young Lutheran leaders in California centered around an annual gathering at Robinwood Church in Huntington Beach. [Richard A. Jensen](/wiki/Richard_A._Jensen "Richard A. Jensen")'s *Touched by the Spirit* published in 1974, played a major role of the Lutheran understanding to the charismatic movement.

In Congregational and Presbyterian churches which profess a traditionally [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinist "Calvinist") or [Reformed theology](/wiki/Reformed_theology "Reformed theology") there are differing views regarding present-day [continuation](/wiki/Continuationism "Continuationism") or [cessation](/wiki/Cessationism "Cessationism") of the gifts (**charismata**) of the Spirit.[[175]](#cite_note-phen-189)[[176]](#cite_note-epidemic-190) Generally, however, Reformed charismatics distance themselves from renewal movements with tendencies which could be perceived as overemotional, such as [Word of Faith](/wiki/Word_of_Faith "Word of Faith"), [Toronto Blessing](/wiki/Toronto_Blessing "Toronto Blessing"), [Brownsville Revival](/wiki/Brownsville_Revival "Brownsville Revival") and [Lakeland Revival](/wiki/Lakeland_Revival "Lakeland Revival"). Prominent Reformed charismatic denominations are the [Sovereign Grace Churches](/wiki/Sovereign_Grace_Churches "Sovereign Grace Churches") and the [Every Nation](/wiki/Every_Nation "Every Nation") Churches in the US, in Great Britain there is the [Newfrontiers](/wiki/Newfrontiers "Newfrontiers") churches and movement, which leading figure is [Terry Virgo](/wiki/Terry_Virgo "Terry Virgo").[[177]](#cite_note-191)

A minority of [Seventh-day Adventists](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church") today are charismatic. They are strongly associated with those holding more ["progressive" Adventist beliefs](/wiki/Progressive_Adventism "Progressive Adventism"). In the early decades of the church charismatic or ecstatic phenomena were commonplace.[[178]](#cite_note-192)[[179]](#cite_note-193)

### Neo-charismatic churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Neo-charismatic churches")]

Main article: [Neo-charismatic churches](/wiki/Neo-charismatic_churches "Neo-charismatic churches")

Neo-charismatic churches are a category of churches in the Christian [Renewal](/wiki/Renewal_(religion) "Renewal (religion)") movement. Neo-charismatics include the [Third Wave](/wiki/Third_Wave_of_the_Holy_Spirit "Third Wave of the Holy Spirit"), but are broader. Now more numerous than Pentecostals (first wave) and charismatics (second wave) combined, owing to the remarkable growth of [postdenominational](/wiki/Postdenominational_churches "Postdenominational churches") and independent charismatic groups.[[180]](#cite_note-NIDPCM-194)

Neo-charismatics believe in and stress the post-Biblical availability of [gifts of the Holy Spirit](/wiki/Gifts_of_the_Holy_Spirit "Gifts of the Holy Spirit"), including [glossolalia](/wiki/Glossolalia "Glossolalia"), healing, and prophecy. They practice laying on of hands and seek the "infilling" of the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity"). However, a specific experience of [baptism with the Holy Spirit](/wiki/Baptism_with_the_Holy_Spirit "Baptism with the Holy Spirit") may not be requisite for experiencing such gifts. No single form, governmental structure, or style of church service characterizes all neo-charismatic services and churches.

Some nineteen thousand denominations, with approximately 295 million individual adherents, are identified as neo-charismatic.[[181]](#cite_note-195)

## Protestant offshoots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Protestant offshoots")]

### Arminianism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Arminianism")]

Main articles: [Arminianism](/wiki/Arminianism "Arminianism") and [Remonstrants](/wiki/Remonstrants "Remonstrants")

See also: [History of the Calvinist–Arminian debate](/wiki/History_of_the_Calvinist%E2%80%93Arminian_debate "History of the Calvinist–Arminian debate")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/1d/James_Arminius_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:James_Arminius_2.jpg)

[Jacobus Arminius](/wiki/Jacobus_Arminius "Jacobus Arminius"), a [Dutch Reformed Church](/wiki/Dutch_Reformed_Church "Dutch Reformed Church") theologian, whose views influenced parts of Protestantism. A small [Remonstrants](/wiki/Remonstrants "Remonstrants") community remains in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands").

[Arminianism](/wiki/Arminianism "Arminianism") is based on [theological](/wiki/Christian_theology "Christian theology") ideas of the [Dutch Reformed](/wiki/Dutch_Reformed "Dutch Reformed") theologian [Jacobus Arminius](/wiki/Jacobus_Arminius "Jacobus Arminius") (1560–1609) and his historic supporters known as [Remonstrants](/wiki/Remonstrants "Remonstrants"). His teachings held to the [five solae](/wiki/Five_solae "Five solae") of the Reformation, but they were distinct from particular teachings of [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther"), [Huldrych Zwingli](/wiki/Huldrych_Zwingli "Huldrych Zwingli"), [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin"), and other [Protestant Reformers](/wiki/Protestant_Reformers "Protestant Reformers"). Jacobus Arminius was a student of [Theodore Beza](/wiki/Theodore_Beza "Theodore Beza") at the Theological University of Geneva. Arminianism is known to some as a [soteriological](/wiki/Soteriological "Soteriological") diversification of [Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism").[[182]](#cite_note-196) However, to others, Arminianism is a reclamation of early Church theological consensus.[[183]](#cite_note-197) Dutch Arminianism was originally articulated in the Remonstrance (1610), a theological statement signed by 45 ministers and submitted to the [States General of the Netherlands](/wiki/States_General_of_the_Netherlands "States General of the Netherlands"). Many Christian denominations have been influenced by Arminian views on the will of man being freed by grace prior to regeneration, notably the [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists") in the 16th century,[[184]](#cite_note-198) the [Methodists](/wiki/Methodists "Methodists") in the 18th century and the [Seventh-day Adventist Church](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church") in the 19th century.

The original beliefs of Jacobus Arminius himself are commonly defined as Arminianism, but more broadly, the term may embrace the teachings of [Hugo Grotius](/wiki/Hugo_Grotius "Hugo Grotius"), [John Wesley](/wiki/John_Wesley "John Wesley"), and others as well. [Classical Arminianism](/wiki/Arminianism#Classical_Arminianism "Arminianism") and [Wesleyan Arminianism](/wiki/Wesleyan-Arminianism "Wesleyan-Arminianism") are the two main schools of thought. Wesleyan Arminianism is often identical with Methodism. The two systems of Calvinism and Arminianism share both history and many doctrines, and the [history of Christian theology](/wiki/History_of_Christianity "History of Christianity"). However, because of their differences over the doctrines of divine [predestination](/wiki/Predestination "Predestination") and election, many people view these schools of thought as opposed to each other. In short, the difference can be seen ultimately by whether God allows His desire to save all to be resisted by an individual's will (in the Arminian doctrine) or if God's grace is irresistible and limited to only some (in Calvinism). Some Calvinists assert that the Arminian perspective presents a synergistic system of Salvation and therefore is not only by grace, while Arminians firmly reject this conclusion. Many consider the theological differences to be crucial differences in doctrine, while others find them to be relatively minor.[[185]](#cite_note-199)

### Pietism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Pietism")]

Main articles: [Pietism](/wiki/Pietism "Pietism") and [Haugean movement](/wiki/Haugean_movement "Haugean movement")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Philipp_Jakob_Spener.jpg/250px-Philipp_Jakob_Spener.jpg)](/wiki/File:Philipp_Jakob_Spener.jpg)

[Philipp Jakob Spener](/wiki/Philipp_Jakob_Spener "Philipp Jakob Spener"), a German pioneer and founder of [Pietism](/wiki/Pietism "Pietism")

[Pietism](/wiki/Pietism "Pietism") was an influential movement within [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") that combined the 17th-century Lutheran principles with the [Reformed](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") emphasis on individual piety and living a vigorous [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") life.[[186]](#cite_note-200)

It began in the late 17th century, reached its zenith in the mid-18th century, and declined through the 19th century, and had almost vanished in America by the end of the 20th century. Pietistic Lutheranism influenced Lutheranism as a whole and resulted in the formation of certain Pietistic Lutheran denominations (such as the [Church of the Lutheran Brethren](/wiki/Church_of_the_Lutheran_Brethren "Church of the Lutheran Brethren") and [Laestadian Lutheran Church](/wiki/Laestadian_Lutheran_Church "Laestadian Lutheran Church")); additionally some of its theological tenets influenced Protestantism generally, inspiring the [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") priest [John Wesley](/wiki/John_Wesley "John Wesley") to begin the [Methodist](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") movement and [Alexander Mack](/wiki/Alexander_Mack "Alexander Mack") to begin the [Schwarzenau Brethren](/wiki/Schwarzenau_Brethren "Schwarzenau Brethren") denomination in the [Anabaptist](/wiki/Anabaptist "Anabaptist") tradition.[[187]](#cite_note-201)

Though Pietism shares an emphasis on personal behavior with the [Puritan](/wiki/Puritan "Puritan") movement, and the two are often confused, there are important differences, particularly in the concept of the role of religion in government.[[188]](#cite_note-202)

### Puritanism, English dissenters and nonconformists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Puritanism, English dissenters and nonconformists")]

Main articles: [Puritans](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans"), [English Dissenters](/wiki/English_Dissenters "English Dissenters"), [Independent (religion)](/wiki/Independent_(religion) "Independent (religion)"), [Nonconformism](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)"), [English Presbyterianism](/wiki/English_Presbyterianism "English Presbyterianism"), [Ecclesiastical separatism](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_separatism "Ecclesiastical separatism"), and [17th-century denominations in England](/wiki/17th-century_denominations_in_England "17th-century denominations in England")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/The_Landing_of_the_Pilgrims_%281877%29_by_Henry_A._Bacon.jpg/250px-The_Landing_of_the_Pilgrims_%281877%29_by_Henry_A._Bacon.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Landing_of_the_Pilgrims_(1877)_by_Henry_A._Bacon.jpg)

[Pilgrims](/wiki/Pilgrims_(Plymouth_Colony) "Pilgrims (Plymouth Colony)") landing at [Plymouth Rock](/wiki/Plymouth_Rock "Plymouth Rock") in [Plymouth, Massachusetts](/wiki/Plymouth,_Massachusetts "Plymouth, Massachusetts"), in 1620

The [Puritans](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans") were a group of English Protestants in the [16th](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_16th_century "Christianity in the 16th century") and [17th centuries](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_17th_century "Christianity in the 17th century"), which sought to purify the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") of what they considered to be Catholic practices, maintaining that the church was only partially reformed. Puritanism in this sense was founded by some of the returning [clergy exiled under Mary I](/wiki/Marian_exiles "Marian exiles") shortly after the accession of [Elizabeth I of England](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England") in 1558, as an activist movement within the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England").

Puritans were blocked from changing the established church from within, and were severely restricted in England by laws controlling the practice of religion. Their beliefs, however, were transported by the emigration of congregations to the Netherlands (and later to New England), and by evangelical clergy to Ireland (and later into Wales), and were spread into lay society and parts of the educational system, particularly certain colleges of the [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge"). The first Protestant sermon delivered in England was in Cambridge, with the pulpit that this sermon was delivered from surviving to today.[[189]](#cite_note-203)[[190]](#cite_note-204) They took on distinctive beliefs about clerical dress and in opposition to the [episcopal](/wiki/Episcopal_polity "Episcopal polity") system, particularly after the 1619 conclusions of the [Synod of Dort](/wiki/Synod_of_Dort "Synod of Dort") they were resisted by the English bishops. They largely adopted [Sabbatarianism](/wiki/Puritan_Sabbatarianism "Puritan Sabbatarianism") in the 17th century, and were influenced by [millennialism](/wiki/Millennialism "Millennialism").

They formed, and identified with various religious groups advocating greater purity of [worship](/wiki/Worship "Worship") and [doctrine](/wiki/Doctrine "Doctrine"), as well as personal and group [piety](/wiki/Pietism "Pietism"). Puritans adopted a [Reformed theology](/wiki/Reformed_theology "Reformed theology"), but they also took note of radical criticisms of Zwingli in Zurich and Calvin in Geneva. In church polity, some advocated for separation from all other Christians, in favor of autonomous [gathered churches](/wiki/Gathered_church "Gathered church"). These separatist and [independent](/wiki/Independent_(religion) "Independent (religion)") strands of Puritanism became prominent in the 1640s. Although the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") (which expanded into the [Wars of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Wars of the Three Kingdoms")) began over a contest for political power between the [King of England](/wiki/King_of_England "King of England") and the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_England "House of Commons of England"), it divided the country along religious lines as [episcopalians](/wiki/Episcopalianism "Episcopalianism") within the Church of England sided with the Crown and Presbyterians and Independents supported *Parliament* (after the defeat of the Royalists, the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") as well as the Monarch were removed from the political structure of the state to create the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England")). The supporters of a [Presbyterian polity](/wiki/Presbyterian_polity "Presbyterian polity") in the [Westminster Assembly](/wiki/Westminster_Assembly "Westminster Assembly") were unable to forge a new English national church, and the Parliamentary [New Model Army](/wiki/New_Model_Army "New Model Army"), which was made up primarily of Independents, under [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") first purged Parliament, then abolished it and established [The Protectorate](/wiki/The_Protectorate "The Protectorate").

[England's trans-Atlantic colonies in the war](/wiki/English_overseas_possessions_in_the_Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "English overseas possessions in the Wars of the Three Kingdoms") followed varying paths depending on their internal demographics. In the older colonies, which included [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia") (1607) and its offshoot [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda") (1612), as well as [Barbados](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados") and [Antigua](/wiki/Antigua "Antigua") in the West Indies (collectively the targets in 1650 of [An Act for prohibiting Trade with the Barbadoes, Virginia, Bermuda and Antego](/wiki/An_Act_for_prohibiting_Trade_with_the_Barbadoes,_Virginia,_Bermuda_and_Antego "An Act for prohibiting Trade with the Barbadoes, Virginia, Bermuda and Antego")), Episcopalians remained the dominant church faction and the colonies remained Royalist 'til conquered or compelled to accept the new political order. In Bermuda, with control of the local [government](/wiki/Government_of_Bermuda "Government of Bermuda") and the *army* (nine infantry companies of Militia plus [coastal artillery](/wiki/Coastal_artillery "Coastal artillery")), the Royalists forced Parliament-backing religious Independents into exile to settle the [Bahamas](/wiki/Bahamas "Bahamas") as the [Eleutheran Adventurers](/wiki/Eleutheran_Adventurers "Eleutheran Adventurers").[[191]](#cite_note-205)[[192]](#cite_note-206)[[193]](#cite_note-207)

Episcopalian was re-established following the [Restoration](/wiki/Stuart_Restoration#Church_of_England_settlement "Stuart Restoration"). A century later, non-conforming Protestants, along with the Protestant refugees from continental Europe, were to be among the primary instigators of the [war of secession](/wiki/American_War_of_Independence "American War of Independence") that led to the founding of the United States of America.

### Neo-orthodoxy and paleo-orthodoxy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Neo-orthodoxy and paleo-orthodoxy")]

Main articles: [Neo-orthodoxy](/wiki/Neo-orthodoxy "Neo-orthodoxy") and [Paleo-orthodoxy](/wiki/Paleo-orthodoxy "Paleo-orthodoxy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Bundesarchiv_Bild_194-1283-23A%2C_Wuppertal%2C_Evangelische_Gesellschaft%2C_Jahrestagung.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_194-1283-23A%2C_Wuppertal%2C_Evangelische_Gesellschaft%2C_Jahrestagung.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_194-1283-23A,_Wuppertal,_Evangelische_Gesellschaft,_Jahrestagung.jpg)

[Karl Barth](/wiki/Karl_Barth "Karl Barth"), often regarded as the greatest Protestant theologian of the 20th century[[194]](#cite_note-McGrath2011-208)[[195]](#cite_note-BrownCollinson2012-209)

A non-fundamentalist rejection of liberal Christianity along the lines of the [Christian existentialism](/wiki/Christian_existentialism "Christian existentialism") of [Søren Kierkegaard](/wiki/S%C3%B8ren_Kierkegaard "Søren Kierkegaard"), who attacked the [Hegelian](/wiki/Right_Hegelians#Hegelian_theologians "Right Hegelians") state churches of his day for "dead orthodoxy", neo-orthodoxy is associated primarily with [Karl Barth](/wiki/Karl_Barth "Karl Barth"), [Jürgen Moltmann](/wiki/J%C3%BCrgen_Moltmann "Jürgen Moltmann"), and [Dietrich Bonhoeffer](/wiki/Dietrich_Bonhoeffer "Dietrich Bonhoeffer"). Neo-orthodoxy sought to counter-act the tendency of liberal theology to make theological accommodations to modern scientific perspectives. Sometimes called "crisis theology", in the existentialist sense of the word crisis, also sometimes called *neo-evangelicalism*, which uses the sense of "evangelical" pertaining to continental European Protestants rather than [American evangelicalism](/wiki/American_evangelicalism "American evangelicalism"). "Evangelical" was the originally preferred label used by Lutherans and Calvinists, but it was replaced by the names some Catholics used to [label](/wiki/Labelling#Labelling_in_argumentation "Labelling") a heresy with the name of its founder.

[Paleo-orthodoxy](/wiki/Paleo-orthodoxy "Paleo-orthodoxy") is a movement similar in some respects to neo-evangelicalism but emphasizing the ancient Christian consensus of the undivided church of the first millennium AD, including in particular the early creeds and church councils as a means of properly understanding the scriptures. This movement is cross-denominational. A prominent theologian in this group is [Thomas Oden](/wiki/Thomas_Oden "Thomas Oden"), a Methodist.

### Christian fundamentalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Christian fundamentalism")]

Main article: [Christian fundamentalism](/wiki/Christian_fundamentalism "Christian fundamentalism")

In reaction to liberal Bible critique, [fundamentalism](/wiki/Fundamentalism "Fundamentalism") arose in the 20th century, primarily in the United States, among those denominations most affected by Evangelicalism. Fundamentalist theology tends to stress [Biblical inerrancy](/wiki/Biblical_inerrancy "Biblical inerrancy") and [Biblical literalism](/wiki/Biblical_literalism "Biblical literalism").[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAmmerman1991-210)

Toward the end of the 20th century, some have tended to confuse evangelicalism and fundamentalism; however, the labels represent very distinct differences of approach that both groups are diligent to maintain, although because of fundamentalism's dramatically smaller size it often gets classified simply as an ultra-conservative branch of evangelicalism.

### Modernism and liberalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Modernism and liberalism")]

Main article: [Liberal Christianity](/wiki/Liberal_Christianity "Liberal Christianity")

Modernism and liberalism do not constitute rigorous and well-defined schools of theology, but are rather an inclination by some writers and teachers to integrate Christian thought into the spirit of the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"). New understandings of history and the natural sciences of the day led directly to new approaches to theology. Its opposition to the fundamentalist teaching resulted in religious debates, such as the [Fundamentalist–Modernist Controversy](/wiki/Fundamentalist%E2%80%93Modernist_Controversy "Fundamentalist–Modernist Controversy") within the [Presbyterian Church in the United States of America](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America "Presbyterian Church in the United States of America") in the 1920s.

## Protestant culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Protestant culture")]

Main article: [Protestant culture](/wiki/Protestant_culture "Protestant culture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Die_protestantische_Ethik_und_der_%27Geist%27_des_Kapitalismus_original_cover.jpg/250px-Die_protestantische_Ethik_und_der_%27Geist%27_des_Kapitalismus_original_cover.jpg)](/wiki/File:Die_protestantische_Ethik_und_der_%27Geist%27_des_Kapitalismus_original_cover.jpg)

[Max Weber](/wiki/Max_Weber "Max Weber")'s *[The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism](/wiki/The_Protestant_Ethic_and_the_Spirit_of_Capitalism "The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism")*

Although the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") was a religious movement, it also had a strong impact on all other aspects of life, including marriage and family, education, the humanities and sciences, the political and social order, the economy, and the arts.[[9]](#cite_note-Karl_Heussi_1956_pp._317–319-15) Protestant churches reject the idea of a celibate priesthood and thus allow their clergy to marry.[[30]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_of_Protestantism-39) Many of their families contributed to the development of intellectual elites in their countries.[[197]](#cite_note-211) Since about 1950, women have entered the ministry in most mainline Protestant churches, and some have assumed leading positions (e.g. [bishops](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop")).

Protestantism has promoted economic growth and entrepreneurship, especially in the period after the [Scientific](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution") and the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution").[[198]](#cite_note-212)[[199]](#cite_note-213) Scholars have identified a positive correlation between the rise of Protestantism and [human capital](/wiki/Human_capital "Human capital") formation,[[200]](#cite_note-214) [work ethic](/wiki/Protestant_work_ethic "Protestant work ethic"),[[201]](#cite_note-215) [economic development](/wiki/Economic_development "Economic development"),[[202]](#cite_note-216) the rise of early [experimental science](/wiki/Experimental_science "Experimental science"),[[203]](#cite_note-217)[[204]](#cite_note-218)[[205]](#cite_note-219) and the development of the [state system](/wiki/Government "Government").[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeckerPfaffRubin2016-220)

As the Reformers wanted all members of the church to be able to read the Bible, education on all levels was strongly encouraged. By the middle of the eighteenth century, the literacy rate in England was about 60 percent, in Scotland 65 percent, and in Sweden 80 percent.[[207]](#cite_note-221) Colleges and universities were founded. For example, the [Puritans](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans") who established [Massachusetts Bay Colony](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay_Colony "Massachusetts Bay Colony") in 1628 founded [Harvard College](/wiki/Harvard_College "Harvard College") only eight years later. About a dozen other colleges followed in the 18th century, including [Yale](/wiki/Yale "Yale") (1701). [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") also became a center of learning.[[208]](#cite_note-222)[[209]](#cite_note-223)

Members of [mainline Protestant](/wiki/Mainline_Protestant "Mainline Protestant") denominations have played [leadership roles in many aspects of American life](/wiki/White_Anglo-Saxon_Protestant "White Anglo-Saxon Protestant"), including politics, business, science, the arts, and education. They founded most of the country's leading institutes of higher education.[[210]](#cite_note-mainline2000-224)

### Visitation Articles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Visitation Articles")]

In the Visitation Articles, also known as the first Protestant Confession of Faith, it declared that "we do not send this forth as a rigid command, lest we set up new papal decrees, but as a history, as a witness of our faith and he expresses the hope that all who hold to the Gospel will thankfully accept it until God shall bring something better." These articles helped serve as a way to document the Lutheran faith and its doctrines. In 1538 and 1545, Luther published new versions of these articles. Luther also added work to The Three Symbols (1538) stating: "I have observed in all histories of the Universal Christian Church that all those who hold to the cardinal doctrines of Jesus Christ have remained sure and steadfast in the Christian faith, and even if they have erred and come short in other respects, they are still preserved." Stating that by following Jesus Christ (specifically with the Lutheran practices in this case) one will be saved.[[211]](#cite_note-225)

### Thought and work ethic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Thought and work ethic")]

See also: [Protestant work ethic](/wiki/Protestant_work_ethic "Protestant work ethic")

The Protestant concept of God and man allows believers to use all their God-given faculties, including the power of reason. That means that they are allowed to explore God's creation and, according to Genesis 2:15, make use of it in a responsible and sustainable way. Thus a cultural climate was created that greatly enhanced the development of the [humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities") and the [sciences](/wiki/Sciences "Sciences").[[212]](#cite_note-226) Another consequence of the Protestant understanding of man is that the believers, in gratitude for their election and redemption in Christ, are to follow God's commandments. Industry, frugality, calling, discipline, and a strong sense of responsibility are at the heart of their moral code.[[213]](#cite_note-227)[[214]](#cite_note-228) In particular, Calvin rejected luxury. Therefore, craftsmen, industrialists, and other businessmen were able to reinvest the greater part of their profits in the most efficient machinery and the most modern production methods that were based on progress in the sciences and technology. As a result, productivity grew, which led to increased profits and enabled employers to pay higher wages. In this way, the economy, the sciences, and technology reinforced each other. The chance to participate in the economic success of technological inventions was a strong incentive to both inventors and investors.[[215]](#cite_note-229)[[216]](#cite_note-230)[[217]](#cite_note-231)[[218]](#cite_note-232) The [Protestant work ethic](/wiki/Protestant_work_ethic "Protestant work ethic") was an important force behind the unplanned and uncoordinated [mass action](/wiki/Mass_action_(sociology) "Mass action (sociology)") that influenced the development of [capitalism](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism") and the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"). This idea is also known as the "Protestant ethic thesis".[[219]](#cite_note-SEP-233)

However, eminent historian [Fernand Braudel](/wiki/Fernand_Braudel "Fernand Braudel") (d. 1985), a leader of the important [Annales School](/wiki/Annales_School "Annales School") wrote, "all historians have opposed this tenuous theory [the Protestant Ethic], although they have not managed to be rid of it once and for all. Yet it is clearly false. The northern countries took over the place that earlier had been so long and brilliantly been occupied by the old capitalist centers of the Mediterranean. They invented nothing, either in technology or business management."[[220]](#cite_note-234) Social scientist [Rodney Stark](/wiki/Rodney_Stark "Rodney Stark") moreover comments that "during their critical period of economic development, these northern centers of capitalism were Catholic, not Protestant—the Reformation still lay well into the future",[[221]](#cite_note-235) while British historian Hugh Trevor-Roper (d. 2003) said, "The idea that large-scale industrial capitalism was ideologically impossible before the Reformation is exploded by the simple fact that it existed."[[222]](#cite_note-236)

[Episcopalians](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)") and [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(U.S.A.) "Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.)"), as well as other [WASPs](/wiki/White_Anglo-Saxon_Protestant "White Anglo-Saxon Protestant"), tend to be considerably wealthier[[223]](#cite_note-THE_EPISCOPALIANS-237) and better educated (having [graduate](/wiki/Academic_degree "Academic degree") and [post-graduate](/wiki/Post-graduate "Post-graduate") degrees per capita) than most other religious groups in [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"),[[224]](#cite_note-238) and are disproportionately represented in the upper reaches of American [business](/wiki/Business "Business"),[[225]](#cite_note-239) [law](/wiki/Law "Law") and [politics](/wiki/Politics "Politics"), especially the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)").[[226]](#cite_note-240) Numbers of the most [wealthy and affluent American families](/wiki/Old_money "Old money") as the [Vanderbilts](/wiki/Vanderbilts "Vanderbilts"), the [Astors](/wiki/Astor_family "Astor family"), [Rockefellers](/wiki/Rockefeller_family "Rockefeller family"), [Du Ponts](/wiki/Du_Pont_family "Du Pont family"), [Roosevelts](/wiki/Roosevelt_family "Roosevelt family"), [Forbes](/wiki/Forbes_family "Forbes family"), [Fords](/wiki/Ford_family "Ford family"), [Whitneys](/wiki/Whitney_family "Whitney family"), [Mellons](/wiki/Mellon_family "Mellon family"), the [Morgans](/wiki/Morgan_family "Morgan family") and Harrimans are [Mainline Protestant](/wiki/Mainline_Protestant "Mainline Protestant") families.[[223]](#cite_note-THE_EPISCOPALIANS-237)[[227]](#cite_note-241)

### Science

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Science")]

See also: [Merton thesis](/wiki/Merton_thesis "Merton thesis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Butler_Library_-_1000px_-_AC.jpg/330px-Butler_Library_-_1000px_-_AC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Butler_Library_-_1000px_-_AC.jpg)

[Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"), an [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") university in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), was initially established by the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England").

Protestantism has had an important influence on science.
