According to the [Merton Thesis](/wiki/Merton_Thesis "Merton Thesis"), there was a positive [correlation](/wiki/Correlation "Correlation") between the rise of English [Puritanism](/wiki/Puritanism "Puritanism") and German [Pietism](/wiki/Pietism "Pietism") on the one hand and early [experimental science](/wiki/Experimental_science "Experimental science") on the other.[[228]](#cite_note-sztompka2003-242) The Merton Thesis has two separate parts: Firstly, it presents a theory that science changes due to an accumulation of observations and improvement in experimental technique and [methodology](/wiki/Methodology "Methodology"); secondly, it puts forward the argument that the popularity of science in 17th-century England and the religious [demography](/wiki/Demography "Demography") of the [Royal Society](/wiki/Royal_Society "Royal Society") (English scientists of that time were predominantly Puritans or other Protestants) can be explained by a [correlation](/wiki/Correlation "Correlation") between Protestantism and the scientific values.[[229]](#cite_note-gregory1998-243) Merton focused on English Puritanism and German Pietism as having been responsible for the development of the [Scientific Revolution](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution") of the 17th and 18th centuries. He explained that the connection between [religious affiliation](/wiki/Religious_affiliation "Religious affiliation") and interest in science was the result of a significant synergy between the [ascetic](/wiki/Ascetic "Ascetic") Protestant values and those of modern science.[[230]](#cite_note-becker1992-244) Protestant values encouraged scientific research by allowing science to identify God's influence on the world—his creation—and thus providing a religious justification for scientific research.[[228]](#cite_note-sztompka2003-242)

According to *Scientific Elite: Nobel Laureates in the United States* by [Harriet Zuckerman](/wiki/Harriet_Zuckerman "Harriet Zuckerman"), a review of American [Nobel Prizes](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") awarded between 1901 and 1972, 72% of American [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prizes "Nobel Prizes") laureates identified a Protestant background.[[231]](#cite_note-Scientific_Elite:_Nobel_Laureates_in_the_United_Statesh-245) Overall, 84% of all the Nobel Prizes awarded to Americans in [Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry"),[[231]](#cite_note-Scientific_Elite:_Nobel_Laureates_in_the_United_Statesh-245) 60% in [Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine"),[[231]](#cite_note-Scientific_Elite:_Nobel_Laureates_in_the_United_Statesh-245) and 59% in [Physics](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics")[[231]](#cite_note-Scientific_Elite:_Nobel_Laureates_in_the_United_Statesh-245) between 1901 and 1972 were won by Protestants.

According to *100 Years of Nobel Prize (2005)*, a review of Nobel Prizes awarded between 1901 and 2000, 65% of [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prizes "Nobel Prizes") Laureates, [have identified Christianity](/wiki/List_of_Christian_Nobel_laureates "List of Christian Nobel laureates") in its various forms as their religious preference (423 prizes).[[232]](#cite_note-Nobel_prize-246) While 32% have identified with Protestantism in its various forms (208 prizes),[[232]](#cite_note-Nobel_prize-246) although Protestants are 12% to 13% of the world's population.

### Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Government")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Evang.svg/120px-Evang.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Evang.svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Kreuz_prot.svg/120px-Kreuz_prot.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Kreuz_prot.svg)

Church flags, as used by German Protestants

During the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), the Church and the worldly authorities were closely related. Martin Luther separated the religious and the worldly realms in principle ([doctrine of the two kingdoms](/wiki/Doctrine_of_the_two_kingdoms "Doctrine of the two kingdoms")).[[233]](#cite_note-247) The believers were obliged to use reason to govern the worldly sphere in an orderly and peaceful way. Luther's doctrine of the [priesthood of all believers](/wiki/Priesthood_of_all_believers "Priesthood of all believers") upgraded the role of laymen in the church considerably. The members of a congregation had the right to elect a minister and, if necessary, to vote for his dismissal (Treatise *On the right and authority of a Christian assembly or congregation to judge all doctrines and to call, install and dismiss teachers, as testified in Scripture*; 1523).[[234]](#cite_note-248) Calvin strengthened this basically democratic approach by including elected laymen ([church elders](/wiki/Church_elder "Church elder"), [presbyters](/wiki/Presbyter "Presbyter")) in his representative church government.[[235]](#cite_note-249) The [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenot "Huguenot") added regional [synods](/wiki/Synod "Synod") and a national synod, whose members were elected by the congregations, to Calvin's system of church self-government. This system was taken over by the other reformed churches[[236]](#cite_note-250) and was adopted by some Lutherans beginning with those in [Jülich-Cleves-Berg](/wiki/United_Duchies_of_J%C3%BClich-Cleves-Berg "United Duchies of Jülich-Cleves-Berg") during the 17th century.

Politically, Calvin favored a mixture of aristocracy and democracy. He appreciated the advantages of [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy"): "It is an invaluable gift, if God allows a people to freely elect its own authorities and overlords."[[237]](#cite_note-251) Calvin also thought that earthly rulers lose their divine right and must be put down when they rise up against God. To further protect the rights of ordinary people, Calvin suggested separating political powers in a system of checks and balances ([separation of powers](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers")). Thus he and his followers resisted political [absolutism](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy") and paved the way for the rise of modern democracy.[[238]](#cite_note-252) Besides England, the Netherlands were, under Calvinist leadership, the freest country in Europe in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. It granted asylum to philosophers like [Baruch Spinoza](/wiki/Baruch_Spinoza "Baruch Spinoza") and [Pierre Bayle](/wiki/Pierre_Bayle "Pierre Bayle"). [Hugo Grotius](/wiki/Hugo_Grotius "Hugo Grotius") was able to teach his natural-law theory and a relatively liberal interpretation of the Bible.[[239]](#cite_note-253)

Consistent with Calvin's political ideas, Protestants created both the English and the American democracies. In seventeenth-century England, the most important persons and events in this process were the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell"), [John Milton](/wiki/John_Milton "John Milton"), [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke"), the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution"), the [English Bill of Rights](/wiki/English_Bill_of_Rights "English Bill of Rights"), and the [Act of Settlement](/wiki/Act_of_Settlement_1701 "Act of Settlement 1701").[[240]](#cite_note-254) Later, the British took their democratic ideals to their colonies, e.g. Australia, New Zealand, and India. In North America, [Plymouth Colony](/wiki/Plymouth_Colony "Plymouth Colony") ([Pilgrim Fathers](/wiki/Pilgrim_Fathers "Pilgrim Fathers"); 1620) and [Massachusetts Bay Colony](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay_Colony "Massachusetts Bay Colony") (1628) practised democratic self-rule and [separation of powers](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers").[[241]](#cite_note-255)[[242]](#cite_note-256)[[243]](#cite_note-257)[[244]](#cite_note-258) These [Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregationalist "Congregationalist") were convinced that the democratic form of government was the will of God.[[245]](#cite_note-259) The [Mayflower Compact](/wiki/Mayflower_Compact "Mayflower Compact") was a [social contract](/wiki/Social_contract "Social contract").[[246]](#cite_note-260)[[247]](#cite_note-261)

### Rights and liberty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Rights and liberty")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/JohnLocke.png/330px-JohnLocke.png)](/wiki/File:JohnLocke.png)

[Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") philosopher [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke") argued for individual conscience, free from state control and helped influence the political ideology of [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") and other [Founding Fathers of the United States](/wiki/Founding_Fathers_of_the_United_States "Founding Fathers of the United States").

Protestants also took the initiative in advocating for [religious freedom](/wiki/Religious_freedom "Religious freedom"). Freedom of conscience had a high priority on the theological, philosophical, and political agendas since Luther refused to recant his beliefs before the Diet of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") at Worms (1521). In his view, faith was a free work of the Holy Spirit and could, therefore, not be forced on a person.[[248]](#cite_note-262) The persecuted Anabaptists and Huguenots demanded freedom of conscience, and they practiced [separation of church and state](/wiki/Separation_of_church_and_state "Separation of church and state").[[249]](#cite_note-263) In the early seventeenth century, Baptists like [John Smyth](/wiki/John_Smyth_(Baptist_minister) "John Smyth (Baptist minister)") and [Thomas Helwys](/wiki/Thomas_Helwys "Thomas Helwys") published tracts in defense of religious freedom.[[250]](#cite_note-264) Their thinking influenced [John Milton](/wiki/John_Milton "John Milton") and [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke")'s stance on tolerance.[[251]](#cite_note-265)[[252]](#cite_note-266) Under the leadership of Baptist [Roger Williams](/wiki/Roger_Williams "Roger Williams"), Congregationalist [Thomas Hooker](/wiki/Thomas_Hooker "Thomas Hooker"), and Quaker [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn"), respectively, [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island"), [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut"), and [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") combined democratic constitutions with freedom of religion. These colonies became safe havens for persecuted religious minorities, including [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews").[[253]](#cite_note-267)[[254]](#cite_note-268)[[255]](#cite_note-269)

The [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"), the [United States Constitution](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution"), and the American [Bill of Rights](/wiki/United_States_Bill_of_Rights "United States Bill of Rights") with its fundamental human rights made this tradition permanent by giving it a legal and political framework.[[256]](#cite_note-270) The great majority of American Protestants, both clergy and laity, strongly supported the independence movement. All major Protestant churches were represented in the First and Second Continental Congresses.[[257]](#cite_note-271) In the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, the American democracy became a model for numerous other countries and regions throughout the world (e.g., Latin America, Japan, and Germany). The strongest link between the American and [French Revolutions](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") was [Marquis de Lafayette](/wiki/Marquis_de_Lafayette "Marquis de Lafayette"), an ardent supporter of the American constitutional principles. The French [Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen](/wiki/Declaration_of_the_Rights_of_Man_and_of_the_Citizen "Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen") was mainly based on Lafayette's draft of this document.[[258]](#cite_note-272) The [Declaration by United Nations](/wiki/Declaration_by_United_Nations "Declaration by United Nations") and [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights") also echo the American constitutional tradition.[[259]](#cite_note-273)[[260]](#cite_note-274)[[261]](#cite_note-275)

Democracy, social-contract theory, separation of powers, religious freedom, separation of church and state—these achievements of the Reformation and early Protestantism were elaborated on and popularized by [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") thinkers. Some of the philosophers of the English, Scottish, German, and Swiss Enlightenment—[Thomas Hobbes](/wiki/Thomas_Hobbes "Thomas Hobbes"), [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke"), [John Toland](/wiki/John_Toland "John Toland"), [David Hume](/wiki/David_Hume "David Hume"), [Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz](/wiki/Gottfried_Wilhelm_Leibniz "Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz"), [Christian Wolff](/wiki/Christian_Wolff_(philosopher) "Christian Wolff (philosopher)"), [Immanuel Kant](/wiki/Immanuel_Kant "Immanuel Kant"), and [Jean-Jacques Rousseau](/wiki/Jean-Jacques_Rousseau "Jean-Jacques Rousseau")—had Protestant backgrounds.[[262]](#cite_note-276) For example, John Locke, whose political thought was based on "a set of Protestant Christian assumptions",[[263]](#cite_note-277) derived the equality of all humans, including the equality of the genders ("Adam and Eve"), from Genesis 1, 26–28. As all persons were created equally free, all governments needed "the [consent of the governed](/wiki/Consent_of_the_governed "Consent of the governed")".[[264]](#cite_note-278)

Also, other human rights were advocated for by some Protestants. For example, [torture](/wiki/Torture "Torture") was abolished in [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") in 1740, [slavery](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery") in Britain in 1834 and in the United States in 1865 ([William Wilberforce](/wiki/William_Wilberforce "William Wilberforce"), [Harriet Beecher Stowe](/wiki/Harriet_Beecher_Stowe "Harriet Beecher Stowe"), [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln")—against Southern Protestants).[[265]](#cite_note-279)[[266]](#cite_note-280) [Hugo Grotius](/wiki/Hugo_Grotius "Hugo Grotius") and [Samuel Pufendorf](/wiki/Samuel_Pufendorf "Samuel Pufendorf") were among the first thinkers who made significant contributions to [international law](/wiki/International_law "International law").[[267]](#cite_note-281)[[268]](#cite_note-282) The [Geneva Convention](/wiki/Geneva_Convention "Geneva Convention"), an important part of humanitarian [international law](/wiki/International_law "International law"), was largely the work of [Henry Dunant](/wiki/Henry_Dunant "Henry Dunant"), a reformed [pietist](/wiki/Pietist "Pietist"). He also founded the [Red Cross](/wiki/Red_Cross "Red Cross").[[269]](#cite_note-283)

### Social teaching

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Social teaching")]

Protestants have founded hospitals, homes for disabled or elderly people, educational institutions, organizations that give aid to developing countries, and other social welfare agencies.[[270]](#cite_note-284)[[271]](#cite_note-285)[[272]](#cite_note-286) In the nineteenth century, throughout the Anglo-American world, numerous dedicated members of all Protestant denominations were active in social reform movements such as the abolition of slavery, prison reforms, and [woman suffrage](/wiki/Woman_suffrage "Woman suffrage").[[273]](#cite_note-287)[[274]](#cite_note-288)[[275]](#cite_note-289) As an answer to the "social question" of the nineteenth century, Germany under Chancellor [Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck") introduced insurance programs that led the way to the [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state") ([health insurance](/wiki/Health_insurance "Health insurance"), [accident insurance](/wiki/Accident_insurance "Accident insurance"), [disability insurance](/wiki/Disability_insurance "Disability insurance"), [old-age pensions](/wiki/Old-age_pension "Old-age pension")). To Bismarck this was "practical Christianity".[[276]](#cite_note-290)[[277]](#cite_note-291) These programs, too, were copied by many other nations, particularly in the Western world.

### Arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Arts")]

Further information: [Reformation § Music and art](/wiki/Reformation#Music_and_art "Reformation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Martin-Luther-Denkmal%2C_Worms.JPG/250px-Martin-Luther-Denkmal%2C_Worms.JPG)](/wiki/File:Martin-Luther-Denkmal,_Worms.JPG)

[Luther Monument in Worms](/wiki/Luther_Monument_(Worms) "Luther Monument (Worms)"), which features some of the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation")'s crucial figures

The arts have been strongly inspired by Protestant beliefs.

[Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther"), [Paul Gerhardt](/wiki/Paul_Gerhardt "Paul Gerhardt"), [George Wither](/wiki/George_Wither "George Wither"), [Isaac Watts](/wiki/Isaac_Watts "Isaac Watts"), [Charles Wesley](/wiki/Charles_Wesley "Charles Wesley"), [William Cowper](/wiki/William_Cowper "William Cowper"), and other authors and composers created well-known church hymns.

Musicians like [Heinrich Schütz](/wiki/Heinrich_Sch%C3%BCtz "Heinrich Schütz"), [Johann Sebastian Bach](/wiki/Johann_Sebastian_Bach "Johann Sebastian Bach"), [George Frideric Handel](/wiki/George_Frideric_Handel "George Frideric Handel"), [Henry Purcell](/wiki/Henry_Purcell "Henry Purcell"), [Johannes Brahms](/wiki/Johannes_Brahms "Johannes Brahms"), [Philipp Nicolai](/wiki/Philipp_Nicolai "Philipp Nicolai") and [Felix Mendelssohn](/wiki/Felix_Mendelssohn "Felix Mendelssohn") composed great works of music.

Prominent painters with Protestant background were, for example, [Albrecht Dürer](/wiki/Albrecht_D%C3%BCrer "Albrecht Dürer"), [Hans Holbein the Younger](/wiki/Hans_Holbein_the_Younger "Hans Holbein the Younger"), [Lucas Cranach the Elder](/wiki/Lucas_Cranach_the_Elder "Lucas Cranach the Elder"), [Lucas Cranach the Younger](/wiki/Lucas_Cranach_the_Younger "Lucas Cranach the Younger"), [Rembrandt](/wiki/Rembrandt "Rembrandt"), and [Vincent van Gogh](/wiki/Vincent_van_Gogh "Vincent van Gogh").

World literature was enriched by the works of [Edmund Spenser](/wiki/Edmund_Spenser "Edmund Spenser"), [John Milton](/wiki/John_Milton "John Milton"), [John Bunyan](/wiki/John_Bunyan "John Bunyan"), [John Donne](/wiki/John_Donne "John Donne"), [John Dryden](/wiki/John_Dryden "John Dryden"), [Daniel Defoe](/wiki/Daniel_Defoe "Daniel Defoe"), [William Wordsworth](/wiki/William_Wordsworth "William Wordsworth"), [Jonathan Swift](/wiki/Jonathan_Swift "Jonathan Swift"), [Johann Wolfgang Goethe](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang Goethe"), [Friedrich Schiller](/wiki/Friedrich_Schiller "Friedrich Schiller"), [Samuel Taylor Coleridge](/wiki/Samuel_Taylor_Coleridge "Samuel Taylor Coleridge"), [Edgar Allan Poe](/wiki/Edgar_Allan_Poe "Edgar Allan Poe"), [Matthew Arnold](/wiki/Matthew_Arnold "Matthew Arnold"), [Conrad Ferdinand Meyer](/wiki/Conrad_Ferdinand_Meyer "Conrad Ferdinand Meyer"), [Theodor Fontane](/wiki/Theodor_Fontane "Theodor Fontane"), [Washington Irving](/wiki/Washington_Irving "Washington Irving"), [Robert Browning](/wiki/Robert_Browning "Robert Browning"), [Emily Dickinson](/wiki/Emily_Dickinson "Emily Dickinson"), [Emily Brontë](/wiki/Emily_Bront%C3%AB "Emily Brontë"), [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens"), [Nathaniel Hawthorne](/wiki/Nathaniel_Hawthorne "Nathaniel Hawthorne"), [Thomas Stearns Eliot](/wiki/Thomas_Stearns_Eliot "Thomas Stearns Eliot"), [John Galsworthy](/wiki/John_Galsworthy "John Galsworthy"), [Thomas Mann](/wiki/Thomas_Mann "Thomas Mann"), [William Faulkner](/wiki/William_Faulkner "William Faulkner"), [John Updike](/wiki/John_Updike "John Updike"), and many others.

## Catholic responses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Catholic responses")]

Main articles: [Anti-Protestantism](/wiki/Anti-Protestantism "Anti-Protestantism"), [Counter-Reformation § Politics](/wiki/Counter-Reformation#Politics "Counter-Reformation"), [Council of Trent](/wiki/Council_of_Trent "Council of Trent"), and [Criticism of Protestantism](/wiki/Criticism_of_Protestantism "Criticism of Protestantism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Matanzas_Inlet_Aerial_view.jpg/250px-Matanzas_Inlet_Aerial_view.jpg)](/wiki/File:Matanzas_Inlet_Aerial_view.jpg)

[Matanzas Inlet](/wiki/Matanzas_Inlet "Matanzas Inlet"), Florida, where Protestant shipwreck [survivors were executed](/wiki/Spanish_assault_on_French_Florida#Massacre_at_Matanzas_Inlet "Spanish assault on French Florida") by [Menéndez](/wiki/Pedro_Men%C3%A9ndez_de_Avil%C3%A9s "Pedro Menéndez de Avilés") "because they had built it there without Your Majesty's permission, and were disseminating the Lutheran religion"

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/La_masacre_de_San_Bartolom%C3%A9%2C_por_Fran%C3%A7ois_Dubois.jpg/250px-La_masacre_de_San_Bartolom%C3%A9%2C_por_Fran%C3%A7ois_Dubois.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_masacre_de_San_Bartolom%C3%A9,_por_Fran%C3%A7ois_Dubois.jpg)

[St. Bartholomew's Day massacre](/wiki/St._Bartholomew%27s_Day_massacre "St. Bartholomew's Day massacre") of French Protestants, 1572

The view of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") is that Protestant denominations cannot be considered churches but rather that they are *ecclesial communities* or *specific faith-believing communities* because their ordinances and doctrines are not historically the same as the Catholic sacraments and dogmas, and the Protestant communities have no sacramental ministerial priesthood[[o]](#cite_note-292) and therefore lack true [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession").[[278]](#cite_note-293)[[279]](#cite_note-AFAC-294) According to Bishop [Hilarion (Alfeyev)](/wiki/Hilarion_(Alfeyev) "Hilarion (Alfeyev)") the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") shares the same view on the subject.[[280]](#cite_note-295)

Contrary to how the Protestant Reformers were often characterized, the concept of a *catholic* or universal Church was not brushed aside during the Protestant Reformation. On the contrary, the visible unity of the *catholic* or *universal church* was seen by the Protestant reformers as an important and essential doctrine of the Reformation. The Magisterial reformers, such as Martin Luther, John Calvin, and Huldrych Zwingli, believed that they were reforming the Catholic Church, which they viewed as having become corrupted.[[p]](#cite_note-296) Each of them took very seriously the charges of schism and innovation, denying these charges and maintaining that it was the Catholic Church that had left them. The Protestant Reformers formed a new and radically different theological opinion on ecclesiology, that the visible Church is "catholic" (lower-case "c") rather than "Catholic" (upper-case "C"). Accordingly, there is not an indefinite number of parochial, congregational or national churches, constituting, as it were, so many ecclesiastical individualities, but one great spiritual republic of which these various organizations form a part,[[q]](#cite_note-297) although they each have very different opinions. This was markedly far-removed from the traditional and historic Catholic understanding that the Catholic Church was the one true Church of Christ.[[r]](#cite_note-298)

Yet, in the Protestant understanding, the *visible church* is not a genus, so to speak, with so many species under it.[[s]](#cite_note-299) In order to justify their departure[[t]](#cite_note-300) from the Catholic Church, Protestants often posited a new argument,[[u]](#cite_note-301) saying that there was no real visible Church with divine authority, only a *spiritual, invisible, and hidden church*—this notion began in the early days of the Protestant Reformation.

Wherever the Magisterial Reformation, which received support from the ruling authorities, took place, the result was a reformed national Protestant church envisioned to be a part of the whole *invisible church*, but disagreeing, in certain important points of doctrine and doctrine-linked practice, with what had until then been considered the normative reference point on such matters,[[v]](#cite_note-302) namely the Papacy and central authority of the Catholic Church. The Reformed churches thus believed in some form of Catholicity, founded on their doctrines of the five solas and a visible [ecclesiastical](/wiki/Ecclesiastical "Ecclesiastical") organization based on the 14th- and 15th-century [Conciliar movement](/wiki/Conciliarism "Conciliarism"), rejecting the [papacy](/wiki/Papacy "Papacy") and [papal infallibility](/wiki/Papal_infallibility "Papal infallibility") in favor of [ecumenical councils](/wiki/Ecumenical_council "Ecumenical council"), but rejecting the latest ecumenical council, the [Council of Trent](/wiki/Council_of_Trent "Council of Trent").[[w]](#cite_note-303) Religious unity therefore became not one of doctrine and identity but one of invisible character, wherein the unity was one of faith in Jesus Christ, not common identity, doctrine, belief, and collaborative action.

There are Protestants,[[x]](#cite_note-304) especially of the [Reformed tradition](/wiki/Reformed_tradition "Reformed tradition"), that either reject or downplay the designation *Protestant* because of the negative idea that the word invokes in addition to its primary meaning, preferring the designation *Reformed*, *Evangelical* or even *Reformed Catholic* expressive of what they call a *Reformed Catholicity* and defending their arguments from the traditional Protestant confessions.[[281]](#cite_note-305)

## Ecumenism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Ecumenism")]

Main article: [Christian ecumenism](/wiki/Christian_ecumenism "Christian ecumenism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Marburger-Religionsgespr%C3%A4ch.jpg/250px-Marburger-Religionsgespr%C3%A4ch.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marburger-Religionsgespr%C3%A4ch.jpg)

The [Marburg Colloquy](/wiki/Marburg_Colloquy "Marburg Colloquy") (1529) was an early attempt at uniting [Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") and [Zwingli](/wiki/Huldrych_Zwingli "Huldrych Zwingli"). It failed as both reformers and their delegations could not agree on the sacrament of the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist"). Similar discussions were held in 1586 during the [Colloquy of Montbéliard](/wiki/Colloquy_of_Montb%C3%A9liard "Colloquy of Montbéliard") and from 1661 to 1663 during the [Syncretistic controversy](/wiki/Syncretistic_controversy "Syncretistic controversy"). Anonymous woodcut, 1557.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/The_1910_World_Missionary_Conference%2C_the_Edinburgh_Missionary_Conference.jpg/250px-The_1910_World_Missionary_Conference%2C_the_Edinburgh_Missionary_Conference.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_1910_World_Missionary_Conference,_the_Edinburgh_Missionary_Conference.jpg)

The Edinburgh Missionary Conference is considered the symbolic starting point of the contemporary ecumenical movement.[[282]](#cite_note-306)

The ecumenical movement has had an influence on [mainline](/wiki/Mainline_(Protestant) "Mainline (Protestant)") churches, beginning at least in 1910 with the [Edinburgh Missionary Conference](/wiki/Edinburgh_Missionary_Conference "Edinburgh Missionary Conference"). Its origins lay in the recognition of the need for cooperation on the mission field in Africa, Asia and Oceania. Since 1948, the [World Council of Churches](/wiki/World_Council_of_Churches "World Council of Churches") has been influential, but ineffective in creating a united church. There are also ecumenical bodies at regional, national and local levels across the globe; but schisms still far outnumber unifications. One, but not the only expression of the ecumenical movement, has been the move to form united churches, such as the [Church of South India](/wiki/Church_of_South_India "Church of South India"), the [Church of North India](/wiki/Church_of_North_India "Church of North India"), the US-based [United Church of Christ](/wiki/United_Church_of_Christ "United Church of Christ"), the [United Church of Canada](/wiki/United_Church_of_Canada "United Church of Canada"), the [Uniting Church in Australia](/wiki/Uniting_Church_in_Australia "Uniting Church in Australia") and the [United Church of Christ in the Philippines](/wiki/United_Church_of_Christ_in_the_Philippines "United Church of Christ in the Philippines") which have rapidly declining memberships. There has been a strong engagement of [Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") churches in the ecumenical movement, though the reaction of individual Orthodox theologians has ranged from tentative approval of the aim of Christian unity to outright condemnation of the perceived effect of watering down Orthodox doctrine.[[283]](#cite_note-307)

A Protestant [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") is held to be valid by the Catholic Church if given with the trinitarian formula and with the intent to baptize. However, as the ordination of Protestant ministers is not recognized due to the lack of [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession") and the disunity from Catholic Church, all other sacraments (except marriage) performed by Protestant denominations and ministers are not recognized as valid. Therefore, Protestants desiring full communion with the Catholic Church are not re-baptized (although they are confirmed) and Protestant ministers who become Catholics may be ordained to the [priesthood](/wiki/Catholic_priesthood "Catholic priesthood") after a period of study.

In 1999, the representatives of [Lutheran World Federation](/wiki/Lutheran_World_Federation "Lutheran World Federation") and Catholic Church signed the [Joint Declaration on the Doctrine of Justification](/wiki/Joint_Declaration_on_the_Doctrine_of_Justification "Joint Declaration on the Doctrine of Justification"), apparently resolving the conflict over the nature of [justification](/wiki/Justification_(theology) "Justification (theology)") which was at the root of the Protestant Reformation, although [Confessional Lutherans](/wiki/Confessional_Lutheran "Confessional Lutheran") reject this statement.[[284]](#cite_note-WELS-JD-308) This is understandable, since there is no compelling authority within them. On 18 July 2006, delegates to the World Methodist Conference voted unanimously to adopt the Joint Declaration.[[285]](#cite_note-309)[[286]](#cite_note-310)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Main article: [Protestantism by country](/wiki/Protestantism_by_country "Protestantism by country")

See also: [Christianity by country](/wiki/Christianity_by_country "Christianity by country")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/St._Peter%27s_Church%2C_Bermuda%2C_Front.jpg/330px-St._Peter%27s_Church%2C_Bermuda%2C_Front.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Peter%27s_Church,_Bermuda,_Front.jpg)

[St. Peter's Church](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Church,_St._George%27s "St. Peter's Church, St. George's") in [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda"), built in 1612, is the oldest surviving Protestant church in the "New World", including the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") and certain Atlantic Ocean islands. It was the first of nine [Parish churches](/wiki/Parish_church "Parish church") established in Bermuda by the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"). Bermuda also has the oldest [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") church outside the British Isles, the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland")'s [Christ Church](/wiki/Christ_Church_in_Warwick "Christ Church in Warwick") (1719).

Estimates for Protestants worldwide vary from 600 million to 1 billion,[[287]](#cite_note-Diamond,_Plattner,_Costopoulos,_2005-311)[[288]](#cite_note-Hillerbrand2-312)[[y]](#cite_note-313) among approximately 2.4 billion Christians.[[24]](#cite_note-WorldChristianDatabase-33)[[289]](#cite_note-World-314)[[290]](#cite_note-Global_Christianity-315)[[291]](#cite_note-316)[[292]](#cite_note-317)[[14]](#cite_note-pewforum1-22)[[8]](#cite_note-Hillerbrand-14)[[293]](#cite_note-Clarke,_Beyer-318)[[294]](#cite_note-319)[[295]](#cite_note-Noll-320)[[z]](#cite_note-321) The main reason for this wide range is the lack of a common agreement among scholars as to which denominations constitute Protestantism. For instance, most sources include [Anabaptism](/wiki/Anabaptism "Anabaptism"), [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists") and [non-denominational Christianity](/wiki/Non-denominational_Christianity "Non-denominational Christianity") as part of Protestantism. However, widely used references like the *World Christian Encyclopedia*, which has been documenting the changing status of World Christianity over the past 120 years, classifies Independent Christians or non-denominational Pentecostals as a separate category from Protestantism.[[296]](#cite_note-:4-322)[[25]](#cite_note-Independents-34)[[297]](#cite_note-Shurden1995-323) As such, in 2026, the [World Christian Database](/wiki/World_Christian_Encyclopedia "World Christian Encyclopedia") reported 638 million historic Protestants and 426 million Independent [non-denominational](/wiki/Non-denominational_Christianity "Non-denominational Christianity") Pentecostals.[[24]](#cite_note-WorldChristianDatabase-33)

In 2010, a total of more than 800 million included 300 million in Sub-Saharan Africa, 260 million in the Americas, 140 million in the Asia-Pacific region, 100 million in Europe and 2 million in Middle East-North Africa.[[14]](#cite_note-pewforum1-22) Protestants account for at least one third of Christians worldwide and more than one tenth of the total human population.[[14]](#cite_note-pewforum1-22) Various estimates put the percentage of Protestants in relation to the total number of world's Christians at 33%,[[293]](#cite_note-Clarke,_Beyer-318) 36%,[[298]](#cite_note-324) 36.7%,[[14]](#cite_note-pewforum1-22) and 40%,[[8]](#cite_note-Hillerbrand-14) while in relation to the world's population at 11.6%[[14]](#cite_note-pewforum1-22) and 13%.[[287]](#cite_note-Diamond,_Plattner,_Costopoulos,_2005-311)

Within Europe, Protestantism remains the most practiced religion in the [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom").[[293]](#cite_note-Clarke,_Beyer-318)[[299]](#cite_note-325) In other historical Protestant strongholds such as Germany, the Netherlands, Switzerland, Latvia, and Estonia, it remains one of the most popular religions.[[300]](#cite_note-326) Although the Czech Republic was the site of [one of the most significant pre-reformation movements](/wiki/Hussites "Hussites"),[[301]](#cite_note-museeprotestant.org-327) there is only a small Protestant population today;[[302]](#cite_note-328)[[303]](#cite_note-329) mainly due to historical reasons like persecution of Protestants by the [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") [Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburgs "Habsburgs"),[[304]](#cite_note-330) restrictions during the [Communist rule](/wiki/Communism "Communism"), and also the ongoing [secularization](/wiki/Secularization "Secularization").[[301]](#cite_note-museeprotestant.org-327) Over the last several decades, religious practice has been declining as secularization has increased.[[293]](#cite_note-Clarke,_Beyer-318)[[305]](#cite_note-331) According to a 2019 study about Religiosity in the European Union in 2019 by [Eurobarometer](/wiki/Eurobarometer "Eurobarometer"), Protestants made up 9% of the [EU](/wiki/EU "EU") population.[[306]](#cite_note-332) According to [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center"), Protestants constituted nearly one fifth (or 18%) of the [continent's Christian population](/wiki/Christianity_in_Europe "Christianity in Europe") in 2010.[[14]](#cite_note-pewforum1-22) Clarke and Beyer estimate that Protestants constituted 15% of all Europeans in 2009, while Noll claims that less than 12% of them lived in Europe in 2010.[[293]](#cite_note-Clarke,_Beyer-318)[[295]](#cite_note-Noll-320)

Changes in worldwide Protestantism over the last century have been significant.[[8]](#cite_note-Hillerbrand-14)[[295]](#cite_note-Noll-320)[[307]](#cite_note-Witte_and_Alexander-333) Since 1900, Protestantism has spread rapidly in Africa, Asia, Oceania and Latin America.[[30]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_of_Protestantism-39)[[287]](#cite_note-Diamond,_Plattner,_Costopoulos,_2005-311)[[307]](#cite_note-Witte_and_Alexander-333) That caused Protestantism to be called a primarily non-Western religion.[[295]](#cite_note-Noll-320)[[307]](#cite_note-Witte_and_Alexander-333) Much of the growth has occurred after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), when [decolonization of Africa](/wiki/Decolonization_of_Africa "Decolonization of Africa") and abolition of [various restrictions against Protestants](/wiki/Anti-Protestantism "Anti-Protestantism") in [Latin American](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") countries occurred.[[287]](#cite_note-Diamond,_Plattner,_Costopoulos,_2005-311) According to one source, Protestants constituted respectively 2.5%, 2%, 0.5% of Latin Americans, Africans and Asians.[[287]](#cite_note-Diamond,_Plattner,_Costopoulos,_2005-311) In 2000, percentage of Protestants on mentioned continents was 17%, more than 27% and 6%, respectively.[[287]](#cite_note-Diamond,_Plattner,_Costopoulos,_2005-311) According to Mark A. Noll, 79% of [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicans "Anglicans") lived in the United Kingdom in 1910, while most of the remainder was found in the United States and across the [British Commonwealth](/wiki/British_Commonwealth "British Commonwealth").[[295]](#cite_note-Noll-320) By 2010, 59% of Anglicans were found in Africa.[[295]](#cite_note-Noll-320) In 2010, more Protestants lived in India than in the UK or Germany, while Protestants in Brazil were as numerous as those in the UK and Germany combined.[[295]](#cite_note-Noll-320) Almost as many lived in each of [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria") and China as in all of Europe.[[295]](#cite_note-Noll-320) China is home to world's largest Protestant minority.[[14]](#cite_note-pewforum1-22)[[aa]](#cite_note-334)

Protestantism is growing in Africa,[[30]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_of_Protestantism-39)[[308]](#cite_note-335)[[309]](#cite_note-TIME_2001-336) Asia,[[30]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_of_Protestantism-39)[[309]](#cite_note-TIME_2001-336)[[310]](#cite_note-337) Latin America,[[309]](#cite_note-TIME_2001-336)[[311]](#cite_note-338) and Oceania,[[30]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_of_Protestantism-39)[[307]](#cite_note-Witte_and_Alexander-333) while declining in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America")[[307]](#cite_note-Witte_and_Alexander-333)[[312]](#cite_note-339) and Europe.[[293]](#cite_note-Clarke,_Beyer-318)[[313]](#cite_note-340) In [France](/wiki/France "France"),[[314]](#cite_note-Jean-Paul_Willaime-341) Protestantism was driven underground from the 1685 [revocation](/wiki/Edict_of_Fontainebleau "Edict of Fontainebleau") of the [Edict of Nantes](/wiki/Edict_of_Nantes "Edict of Nantes") until shortly before the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), its adherents are now claimed to be stable in number.[[314]](#cite_note-Jean-Paul_Willaime-341) According to some, [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") is another country to see a Protestant growth.[[315]](#cite_note-342)[[316]](#cite_note-343)[[317]](#cite_note-344)

The United States is home to approximately 20% of the world's Protestants.[[14]](#cite_note-pewforum1-22) According to a 2019 study, the Protestant share of U.S. population dropped to 43%, ending its historical status as religion of the majority.[[318]](#cite_note-345)[[319]](#cite_note-Nones_of_the_Rise2-346)[[320]](#cite_note-347)[[321]](#cite_note-348) The decline is attributed mainly to the dropping membership of the [Mainline Protestant](/wiki/Mainline_Protestant "Mainline Protestant") churches,[[322]](#cite_note-Nones_of_the_Rise-349)[[323]](#cite_note-350) while [Evangelical Protestant](/wiki/Evangelical_Protestant "Evangelical Protestant") and [Black churches](/wiki/Black_church "Black church") are stable or continue to grow.[[322]](#cite_note-Nones_of_the_Rise-349)[[324]](#cite_note-351)[[325]](#cite_note-352)

Protestants have been projected to form a slim majority of the world's total Christian population by 2050 if existing trends continue.[[326]](#cite_note-:3-353)[[ab]](#cite_note-354) According to other experts such as Hans J. Hillerbrand, Protestants will be as numerous as Catholics.[[327]](#cite_note-:2-355) According to [Peter L. Berger](/wiki/Peter_L._Berger "Peter L. Berger"), popular Protestantism[[ac]](#cite_note-356) is the most dynamic religious movement in the contemporary world, alongside [resurgent Islam](/wiki/Islamic_revival "Islamic revival").[[20]](#cite_note-books.google.com-29)

In 2010, the largest Protestant denominational families were historically Pentecostal denominations (11%), Anglican (11%), Lutheran (10%), Baptist (9%), [United and uniting churches](/wiki/United_and_uniting_churches "United and uniting churches") (unions of different denominations) (7%), Presbyterian or Reformed (7%), Methodist (3%), Adventist (3%), Congregationalist (1%), [Brethren](/wiki/Plymouth_Brethren "Plymouth Brethren") (1%), [The Salvation Army](/wiki/The_Salvation_Army "The Salvation Army") (<1%) and [Moravian](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church") (<1%). Other denominations accounted for 38% of Protestants.[[14]](#cite_note-pewforum1-22)

## Explanatory notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protestantism&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Explanatory notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** Protestantism is typically classified as a division of [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") defined by opposition to the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), though [Eastern Protestant](/wiki/Eastern_Protestant_Christianity "Eastern Protestant Christianity") denominations have developed in protest of the [Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") instead.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** Some movements such as the [Hussites](/wiki/Hussites "Hussites") or the [Lollards](/wiki/Lollards "Lollards") are also considered Protestant today, although their origins date back to centuries before the launch of the Reformation. Others, such as the [Waldensians](/wiki/Waldensians "Waldensians"), were later incorporated into another branch of Protestantism; in this case, the Reformed branch.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** Especially in German contexts, [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony") is often described as the "motherland of the Reformation" ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Mutterland der Reformation*).
4. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** At the time Germany and the surrounding region was fragmented into [numerous states](/wiki/States_of_the_Holy_Roman_Empire "States of the Holy Roman Empire") of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"). Areas which turned Protestant were primarily located in northern, central and eastern areas of the Empire.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-11)** Several states of the Holy Roman Empire adopted Calvinism, including the [County Palatine of the Rhine](/wiki/Electoral_Palatinate "Electoral Palatinate").
6. **[^](#cite_ref-13)** For further information, see [English Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation"). In this article, Anglicanism is considered a branch of Protestantism as a part of movements derived directly from the 16th century Reformation. While today the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") often considers itself to be a *[via media](/wiki/Via_media "Via media")* between Protestantism and the Catholic Church, until the rise of the [Oxford Movement](/wiki/Oxford_Movement "Oxford Movement") in the 1830s the church generally considered itself to be Protestant. (Neill, Stephen. *Anglicanism* Pelican 1960, pp. 170, 259–260)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-19)** In 2011, the Pew Research Center estimated that there were 800,640,000 Protestants. Of these, 61.8%, or 494,795.520 people, were members of the largest Protestant denominational families. This includes: 10.8 Historical pentecostal churches (86,469,120 people),10.6% (84,867,840 people) Anglican, 9.7% (77,662,080 people) Lutheran, 9% (72,057,600 people) Baptist, 7.5% (60,048,000 people) Presbyterian/Reformed/Congregational (7% Presbyterian/Reformed, or 56,044,800 people and 0.5%, or 4,003,200 people, Congregational), 7.2% (57,646,080 people) United Church, 3.4% (27,221,760 people) Methodist, 2.7% (21,617,280 people) Adventist, 0.5% (4,003,200 people) Brethren, 0.3% (2,401,920 people) Salvation Army and 0.1% (80,0640 people) Moravians.[[12]](#cite_note-Pew_Research_Center's_Forum_on_Religion_and_Public_Life_21,_70-18)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-21)** This branch was first called *Calvinism* by Lutherans who opposed it, but many find the word *Reformed* to be more descriptive.[[13]](#cite_note-Hägglund_2007-20) It includes [Presbyterianism](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism"), [Congregationalism](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church"), many of [united and uniting churches](/wiki/United_and_uniting_churches "United and uniting churches"), as well as historic [Continental Reformed churches](/wiki/Continental_Reformed_church "Continental Reformed church") in France, Switzerland, the Netherlands, Germany, Hungary, and elsewhere.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-28)** A flexible term; defined as all forms of Protestantism with the notable exception of the historical denominations deriving from the Protestant Reformation.
10. **[^](#cite_ref-86)** In the end, while the Reformation emphasis on Protestants reading the Scriptures was one factor in the development of literacy, the impact of printing itself, the wider availability of printed works at a cheaper price, and the increasing focus on education and learning as key factors in obtaining a lucrative post, were also significant contributory factors.[[76]](#cite_note-Pettegree543-85)
11. **[^](#cite_ref-89)** In the first decade of the Reformation, Luther's message became a movement, and the output of religious pamphlets in Germany was at its height.[[78]](#cite_note-PettegreeHall786-88)
12. **[^](#cite_ref-133)** Finland's State Church was the [Church of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden") until 1809. As an autonomous Grand Duchy under Russia 1809–1917, Finland retained the Lutheran State Church system, and a state church separate from Sweden, later named the [Evangelical Lutheran Church of Finland](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_of_Finland "Evangelical Lutheran Church of Finland"), was established. It was detached from the state as a separate judicial entity when the new church law came to force in 1869. After Finland had gained independence in 1917, religious freedom was declared in the constitution of 1919 and a separate law on religious freedom in 1922. Through this arrangement, the Evangelical Lutheran Church of Finland lost its position as a state church but gained a constitutional status as a national church alongside the [Finnish Orthodox Church](/wiki/Finnish_Orthodox_Church "Finnish Orthodox Church"), whose position, however, is not codified in the constitution.
13. **[^](#cite_ref-175)** For example, the [followers of Thomas Müntzer](/wiki/M%C3%BCnster_Rebellion "Münster Rebellion") and [Balthasar Hubmaier](/wiki/Balthasar_Hubmaier "Balthasar Hubmaier").
14. **[^](#cite_ref-184)** Primarily in the United States, where Protestants are usually placed in one of two categories—[Mainline](/wiki/Mainline_Protestant "Mainline Protestant") or Evangelical.
15. **[^](#cite_ref-292)** this varies among Protestants today. In Sweden, the bishops switched to Lutheranism during the Reformation and there was no break in ordinations. See [Apostolic succession in Sweden](/wiki/Apostolic_succession#Lutheran_claims_to_apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession") for more on this. Today, as a result of shared ordinations, the entire [Porvoo Communion](/wiki/Porvoo_Communion "Porvoo Communion") can trace an unbroken chain of Archbishop-level ordinations going back to before the Reformation through the Swedish line. However, today Rome does not accept these ordinations as valid not because there was a break in the chain, but rather because the occurred apart from papal permission.
16. **[^](#cite_ref-296)** For more on this, see [crypto-paganism](/wiki/Crypto-paganism "Crypto-paganism") and the [Great Apostasy](/wiki/Great_Apostasy#Overview "Great Apostasy"). In some areas, pagan Europeans were forced to adopt Christianity at least outwardly, such as after being defeated in battle by Christians. However, outlawing their paganism did not just make it go away. Rather, it persisted as crypto-paganism. For example, [Philip Melanchthon](/wiki/Philip_Melanchthon "Philip Melanchthon"), in his 1537 [Apology of the Augsburg Confession](/wiki/Apology_of_the_Augsburg_Confession "Apology of the Augsburg Confession") identified the mechanical character of [ex opere operato](/wiki/Ex_opere_operato "Ex opere operato") sacraments as being a form of pagan [deterministic philosophy](/wiki/Deterministic_system_(philosophy) "Deterministic system (philosophy)").
17. **[^](#cite_ref-297)** This is the position of the Protestants who believe the church is visible. For those who think the church is invisible, organizations are irrelevant, as only individual sinners can be saved.
18. **[^](#cite_ref-298)** See [Ecclesiology of Augustine of Hippo](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo#Ecclesiology "Augustine of Hippo") for an example of a church father who discussed the invisible church.
19. **[^](#cite_ref-299)** This is a reference to the [Marks of the Church](/wiki/Marks_of_the_Church_(Protestantism) "Marks of the Church (Protestantism)") in Reformed theology. It is thus you may think of the State, but the visible church is a *totum integrale*, it is an empire, with an ethereal emperor, rather than a visible one. The churches of the various nationalities constitute the provinces of this empire; and though they are so far independent of each other, yet they are so one, that membership in one is membership in all, and separation from one is separation from all.... This conception of the church, of which, in at least some aspects, we have practically so much lost sight, had a firm hold of the Scottish theologians of the seventeenth century. James Walker in *The Theology of Theologians of Scotland*. (Edinburgh: Rpt. Knox Press, 1982) Lecture iv. pp. 95–96.
20. **[^](#cite_ref-300)** At least at first, Protestants did not depart per se. Rather, they were excommunicated such as in the 1520 *[Exsurge Domine](/wiki/Exsurge_Domine "Exsurge Domine")* and the 1521 *[Edict of Worms](/wiki/Edict_of_Worms "Edict of Worms")*. Some Protestants avoided excommunication by living as [crypto-Protestants](/wiki/Crypto-Protestant "Crypto-Protestant").
21. **[^](#cite_ref-301)** Some Protestants claim the church is visible today, this is a matter of dispute.
22. **[^](#cite_ref-302)** The assertion of papal supremacy varied through history. For example, in 381 the [First Council of Constantinople](/wiki/First_Council_of_Constantinople "First Council of Constantinople") recognized the sees of Rome and Constantinople as being equal in authority. Papal supremacy continued to evolve after the Reformation with the [First Vatican Council](/wiki/First_Vatican_Council "First Vatican Council").
23. **[^](#cite_ref-303)** Lutherans did not completely reject Trent. In fact, some attended it, although they were not given a vote. Instead, [Martin Chemnitz](/wiki/Martin_Chemnitz "Martin Chemnitz") on the basis that all councils are subject to examination, wrote the *[Examination of the Council of Trent](/wiki/Examination_of_the_Council_of_Trent "Examination of the Council of Trent")* in which some parts of Trent were accepted and others dissented from.
24. **[^](#cite_ref-304)** In history, Catholic sympathizing Protestants were termed [crypto-papists](/wiki/Crypto-papist "Crypto-papist") and lived as such because Catholicism was illegal in some areas under the legal principle of *[cuius regio, eius religio](/wiki/Cuius_regio,_eius_religio "Cuius regio, eius religio")*. However, outlawing Catholics did not always force them to emigrate. Instead, they remained continued to influence the dominant church in their area.
25. **[^](#cite_ref-313)** Estimates vary considerably, from 400 up to more than a billion. Hans Hillerbrand estimated a total 2004 Protestant population of 833,457,000,[[23]](#cite_note-books.google.pl-32) while the World Christian Database estimated 637,856,000 Protestants and 426,370,000 Independents (mostly non-denominational Pentecostals) in early 2026.[[24]](#cite_note-WorldChristianDatabase-33)
26. **[^](#cite_ref-321)** Current sources are in general agreement that Christians make up about 33% of the world's population—slightly over 2.4 billion adherents in mid-2015.
27. **[^](#cite_ref-334)** Estimates for China vary in dozens of millions. Nevertheless, in comparison to the other countries, there is no disagreement that China has the most numerous Protestant minority.
28. **[^](#cite_ref-354)** Magisterial Protestant, Independent, Anabaptist and Anglican parties are understood as Protestant as stated previously in the article, as well as in the book: *Statistics for the P, I and A megablocs are often combined because they overlap so much-hence the order followed here.*
29. **[^](#cite_ref-356)** A flexible term; defined as all forms of Protestantism with the notable exception of the historical denominations deriving from the Protestant Reformation.

"Wesley also taught that Christians experience several distinct acts of God's grace as they grow into the image of Christ. Two of these acts of grace are justification and entire sanctification. Justification occurs when a sinner is forgiven, regenerated, and made right with God. Entire sanctification happens when the carnal nature within a Christian is finally destroyed, and the believer is then enabled to love God with the whole heart, soul, mind, and strength. Entire sanctification makes Christians perfect in their love for God, but in no other way. Their hearts are so filled with the love of God that there is no room for a contrary affection. Hence they are sinless in that while in this state they do not willingly violate the known law of God. Although believers' hearts are filled with love and freed from sin, sanctified people still suffer the effects of sin on their minds and bodies. They are not free from errors in judgment or mistakes in action. Thus, they continually need the blood of Christ to cleanse them from these accidental "fallings short of the glory of God.""

"Also, for Methodists full salvation involves not only justification by faith, but repentance and holy living as well. Whereas in Lutheran theology the central doctrine and focus of all our worship and life is justification by grace through faith, for Methodists the central focus has always been holy living and the striving for perfection. Wesley gave the analogy of a house. He said repentance is the porch. Faith is the door. But holy living is the house itself. Holy living is true religion. "Salvation is like a house. To get into the house you first have to get on the porch (repentance) and then you have to go through the door (faith). But the house itself—one's relationship with God—is holiness, holy living" (Joyner, paraphrasing Wesley, 3)."

"It is clear that, in rejecting Roman Catholic doctrine on this point, Cranmer has also rejected Luther's views and adopted Calvin's position. The sacrament is not merely a symbol of what takes place in the heart, but neither is it the physical eating of the body of Christ. This must be so, because the body of Christ is in heaven and therefore our participation in it can only be spiritual. Only the believers are the true partakers of the body and blood of Christ, for the unbelievers eat and drink no more than bread and wine—and condemnation upon themselves, for the profanation of the Lord's Table. These views are reflected in the Thirty-nine articles, of which the twenty-eighth says that "the Body of the Christ is given, taken, and eaten, in the Supper, only after an heavently and spiritual manner. The next article says of the wicked that "in no wise are they partakers of Christ," although "to their condemnation [they] do eat and rink the sign or Sacrament of so great a thing." This marked Calvinistic influence would prove very significant for the history of Christianity in England during the seventeenth century"

"In addition to his emphasis on Bible reading and the introduction to the *Book of Common Prayer*, other media through which Cranmer sought to catechize the English people were the introduction of the First Book of Homilies and the 39 Articles of Religion. Together with the *Book of Common Prayer* and the Forty-Two Articles (which were later reduced to thirty-nine), the Book of Homilies stands as one of the essential texts of the Edwardian Reformation, and they all helped to define the shape of Anglicanism then, and in the subsequent centuries. More so, the Articles of Religion, whose primary shape and content were given by Archbishop Cranmer and Bishop Ridley in 1553 (and whose final official form was ratified by Convocation, the Queen, and Parliament in 1571), provided a more precise interpretation of Christian doctrine to the English people. According to John H. Rodgers, they "constitute the formal statements of the accepted, common teaching put forth by the Church of England as a result of the Reformation.""

232. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-Nobel_prize_246-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-Nobel_prize_246-1) Baruch A. Shalev, *[100 Years of Nobel Prizes](https://books.google.com/books?id=3jrbmL-DgZQC) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20200523003140/https://books.google.com/books?id=3jrbmL-DgZQC) 23 May 2020 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")* (2003), Atlantic Publishers & Distributors, p. 57: between 1901 and 2000 reveals that 654 Laureates belong to 28 different religion Most 65% have identified Christianity in its various forms as their religious preference.
     While separating Catholics from Protestants among Christians proved difficult in some cases, available information suggests that more Protestants were involved in the scientific categories and more Catholics were involved in the Literature and Peace categories.
     Atheists, agnostics, and freethinkers constitute 11% of total Nobel Prize winners; but in the category of Literature, these preferences rise sharply to about 35%. A striking fact involving religion is the high number of Laureates of the Jewish faith—over 20% of total Nobel Prizes (138); including: 17% in Chemistry, 26% in Medicine and Physics, 40% in Economics and 11% in Peace and Literature each. The numbers are especially startling in light of the fact that only some 14 million people (0.02% of the world's population) are Jewish. By contrast, only 5 Nobel Laureates have been of the Muslim faith—1% of total number of Nobel prizes awarded—from a population base of about 1.2 billion (20% of the world's population)
